Directly beneath the header, a slim breadcrumb row sits on a white background, followed by a full-width event hero that visually mirrors the marketing hero: large photographic background, centered event headline, minimal supporting text, and a prominent pill-shaped red registration CTA in the central band. Below the hero, content flows in a linear top-to-bottom fashion but uses a two-column layout within the centered 12‑column grid: the left column (about 7–8/12 of the width) is reserved for event information (metadata, description, pricing, addons), while the right column (about 4–5/12) is a vertically stacked registration card housing the toggle, form, payment selector, price summary, and confirmation/error states.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the fixed global header and full-bleed hero pattern from the mockup. Directly beneath the header, a slim breadcrumb row sits on a white background, followed by a full-width event hero that visually mirrors the marketing hero: large photographic background, centered event headline, minimal supporting text, and a prominent pill-shaped red registration CTA in the central band. Below the hero, content flows in a linear top-to-bottom fashion but uses a two-column layout within the centered 12‑column grid: the left column (about 7–8/12 of the width) is reserved for event information (metadata, description, pricing, addons), while the right column (about 4–5/12) is a vertically stacked registration card housing the toggle, form, payment selector, price summary, and confirmation/error states.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header remains relatively shallow and fixed at the top. The event hero occupies roughly 50–60% of the initial viewport height, giving the event title and primary registration CTA strong prominence while still allowing a hint of the registration card to be visible just below the fold on standard laptop screens. Below the hero, the content container uses generous vertical spacing (48–64px) between major blocks: metadata panel, description, pricing, and addons on the left; registration card sections on the right separated by 24–32px internal padding. Horizontally, all main content is aligned to a central max-width container; the hero’s text block uses the central 40–50% of the page width, while the left/right columns use a 7/5 or 8/4 split within the 12‑column grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page preserves the mockup’s moderately spacious feel: short, impactful hero copy and a single dominant CTA, followed by well-separated content sections that never feel cramped. Interactive components (form fields, toggles, selectors) use generous padding (8–16px vertically, 16–24px horizontally) and comfortable vertical spacing (16–24px between input groups). Information-heavy areas like pricing tiers and addons are structured into visually distinct blocks or lists with clear headings and 16–24px internal spacing, ensuring scannability without overwhelming the user. Overall density is calibrated to keep focus on the registration path while making supplementary event information easy to browse.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the F/Z pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left logo in the global header, moves through the main navigation and to the right-aligned site search, then drops to the left-aligned breadcrumb under the header. From there, attention is pulled down to the center of the hero by the large event title and then to the bold red hero CTA. As the user scrolls, the right-column registration card becomes the primary focus for action, with the registration toggle and form fields leading down to the price summary and final submit button. Meanwhile, the left column supports a secondary reading path: metadata, description, pricing tiers, and addons. Confirmation and error states are designed to interrupt this flow clearly but cleanly, appearing in the same visual lanes (hero/registration card or full-width alerts) rather than as unrelated pop-ups.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header and navigation, a slim breadcrumb row sits on a white background, anchored to the left within the central content width. Immediately beneath, a full-bleed hero image related to indoor climbing fills about half the initial viewport height; in its central 40–50% width, a vertically centered text block presents the event name in large, bold type, a short one- or two-line subheading, and a strong red pill-shaped “Register” button aligned center, echoing the marketing hero’s membership CTA. As the page scrolls past the hero, the layout resolves into a two-column composition within a centered container: on the left, a sequence of generous, card-like sections presents the event metadata, a detailed description, pricing tiers, and optional addons; on the right, a unified registration card with a subtle elevation holds the registration toggle at the top, followed by the form fields and payment selector, and ending with a compact but visually strong price summary and final red CTA anchored at the bottom of the card, often staying in view via sticky behavior on taller screens. Error and confirmation states reuse these structural zones—the hero area for load failures and the right-hand card for registration outcomes—so the entire experience feels cohesive, modern, and purpose-built around guiding the user smoothly from understanding the event to completing registration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader appears as a semi-transparent white overlay covering the entire content region below the fixed global header. It stretches full-width from the left edge of the viewport to the right, and vertically from just beneath the header (including breadcrumb, hero, and both columns) down to the bottom of the viewport. A single, medium-sized loading indicator (spinner or similar) is centered both horizontally and vertically in this overlay. The underlying hero image, event details, and registration card remain faintly visible but dimmed, clearly indicating that the structure of the page is present but temporarily inactive while data is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay beneath the global header, occupying the full remaining viewport; sits above all other components with a high z-index and remains fixed while the page underneath would scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is positioned directly below the fixed global header, on a solid white background that clearly separates it from the image-based hero below. It is left-aligned within the central content container, occupying roughly the left 60–70% of that width as a single horizontal line of small-to-regular sized text links, with subtle separators between levels. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the header and the breadcrumb, and another 16–24px of whitespace between the bottom of the breadcrumb and the top edge of the hero image, reinforcing a clean, tiered hierarchy at the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that scrolls away naturally with the page; it does not stick or overlap with the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event summary header occupies a full-bleed hero band immediately below the breadcrumb. A rich, photographic background related to indoor climbing fills the entire width of the viewport. Within this background, a central content column (matching the site’s max-width container) is used; in the middle of that column, a vertically centered text block spans about 40–50% of the page width and is aligned to the center horizontally. The event title appears as a large, multi-line headline with generous line spacing, stacked above a concise one- or two-line subheading if present. Directly beneath the text, a bold pill-shaped red button labeled for registration is centered and sized to roughly the width of a medium-to-large button (e.g., 200–260px), with 16–18px vertical padding and 32px horizontal padding. There is ample negative space around this block, with the edges of the hero image visible to the left and right, so the text and button remain the focal point without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero section spanning full-width; it scrolls with the page and sits directly under the breadcrumb, without sticking, but defines the visual top of the event content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event description section appears in the left column of the main content area, directly below the event metadata panel and aligned to the same left margin within the central container. It occupies about 60–65% of the total page width on desktop (7–8 of 12 grid columns) and stretches down as a vertically stacked block of text. At its top, a clear section heading (e.g., “About this event”) is left-aligned and separated from the metadata panel above by roughly 32–48px of whitespace. Below the heading, body copy flows in well-spaced paragraphs with comfortable line length (around 60–75 characters per line) and 16–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs. There is at least 32px of whitespace below the last paragraph before the next section (pricing and tiers), maintaining the airy, readable quality from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the left content column that scrolls normally; it does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event metadata panel is the first block in the left column below the hero, aligned with the hero’s central content container and the rest of the main content. It spans about 60–65% of the page width on larger screens, taking up 7–8 grid columns aligned to the left. Visually, it appears as a low-elevation card or structured block with 24–32px of internal padding, containing key details such as date, time, location, skill level, and capacity. These details are organized into one or two rows: either a horizontal row of labeled items (each label in smaller, uppercase text above its value) or two columns on wide screens, with comfortable 16–24px spacing between items. There is about 48px of vertical space separating the bottom of the hero from the top of this panel, and another 32–40px separating it from the description section below, creating a clear hierarchy from high-level summary to supporting details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the left content column; scrolls with the page and does not overlap with the registration card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing and tiers section sits in the left column directly below the event description, aligned to the same left margin and occupying the same width (about 60–65% of the page). It is structured as a clearly labeled section with a heading like “Pricing & Tiers” separated from the description by roughly 32–48px of whitespace. Inside, pricing options are presented as a vertical list of tiers, each in its own horizontally full-width row or compact card, with the tier name on the left and price on the right. Each tier item uses 16–24px of vertical padding and is separated from the next by 8–16px gaps or subtle dividers, keeping density moderate and scannable. Any notes or eligibility requirements appear as smaller text beneath each tier, indented slightly or aligned with the tier label. The entire section has a bottom margin of about 32–40px before the addons list to preserve breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the left content column; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The addons list appears as the last major content block in the left column, directly below the pricing and tiers section, maintaining the same width and left alignment. A clear heading (e.g., “Optional Add-ons”) introduces the section, separated from the pricing above by about 32–40px. Below the heading, each addon is represented as a selectable row or compact card spanning the left column width, with the addon name and short description on the left and its price or status on the right. Selection controls (checkboxes or pill-style toggles) are placed at the far left of each row, with 16–24px horizontal padding and 12–16px vertical padding inside each selectable area. There is consistent 8–16px spacing between addon rows, resulting in a moderately dense but clearly structured list that mirrors the tidy, pill-based controls from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static selection list in the left column; selections here influence the price summary in the right column but the list itself scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration toggle sits at the top of the right-hand registration card, aligned to the right column which occupies about 35–40% of the overall content width (4–5 grid columns). The toggle is horizontally centered within this card and appears as a pill-shaped segmented control (e.g., “Member” / “Guest” or “Individual” / “Team”), spanning roughly 70–80% of the card’s width. Each segment has ample horizontal padding (16–24px) and about 12–16px of vertical padding, with a clear active state that uses the brand red or a bold fill while inactive options remain outlined or subdued. There is about 24–32px of padding between the top edge of the card and the toggle, and about 24px of space from the bottom of the toggle to the first form field, signposting the toggle as the initial step in the registration flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the top of the right column’s registration card, which may use sticky behavior on larger screens so that the toggle remains near the top of the viewport as the left column scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration form occupies the main vertical body of the right-hand registration card, directly beneath the registration toggle and above the payment selector and price summary. It is left-aligned within the card with 24–32px of side padding, and spans the full width of the right column (minus padding). Fields are arranged in a single-column stack for clarity, with occasional two-column pairs (e.g., first/last name) on wider screens as long as they fit comfortably within the card. Each form field group includes a label above the input, a well-sized input with 10–14px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding, and 8–12px vertical spacing between consecutive fields. There is about 24–32px of vertical space between logical groups of fields (e.g., personal info vs. participant details), and roughly 32px separating the last form field from the payment method selector below, keeping the vertical rhythm consistent and easy to follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives within the scrollable right column card; on larger screens, the top of the card (including the start of the form) can remain sticky within the viewport, but the form itself scrolls within the page rather than within an inner scroll area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration price summary sits at the bottom of the right-hand registration card, beneath the payment method selector, forming a compact but visually strong block that anchors the user’s attention on the final cost and action. It stretches the full inner width of the card (with 24–32px side padding) and is separated from the payment selector by about 24–32px. Within this block, a small, subtle breakdown (e.g., base price, addons, discounts) is aligned to the left in one or two short lines, while the final total amount is presented in larger, bolder text aligned either to the right or centered below the breakdown. Directly under the totals, a full-width pill-shaped red button spans nearly the entire interior width of the card, with generous 14–18px vertical padding, clearly marked as the final “Complete Registration” or similar CTA. The block is separated from the bottom of the card by 24–32px of padding, making the price summary and CTA feel anchored but not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the bottom of the right column’s registration card; on taller screens, the entire card (including this summary) can use sticky positioning so that the price and CTA remain visible while scrolling the event details in the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method selector is located within the right-hand registration card, directly below the main registration form fields and above the registration price summary. It occupies the full width of the card interior (minus side padding) and is introduced by a concise label such as “Payment method.” Beneath the label, a row or short vertical stack of pill-style or card-style options (e.g., credit card, bank transfer, digital wallet) is displayed, each option having 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. On wider screens, methods may appear side-by-side in a single row, while on narrower screens they stack vertically. The active selection uses a filled or outlined state with brand colors to match other controls. There is 16–24px spacing between the last form field and this selector, and another 24–32px between the selector and the price summary below, maintaining a tidy separation between steps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the registration card; it does not float independently but moves as part of the right column, benefiting from any sticky behavior of the card itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful registration, the confirmation state takes over the content area of the right-hand registration card, replacing the form, payment selector, and price summary within the same footprint. The card’s position and width remain identical—about 35–40% of the page on desktop, aligned to the right and vertically alongside the left column content—but its interior shifts to a status display. At the top, a prominent success icon or badge sits next to or above a bold, short success headline (e.g., “You’re registered!”), followed by a brief summary of the registration details in smaller text. There is 24–32px padding around all sides, and the content is vertically stacked with 16–24px spacing between headline, summary, and any secondary buttons (e.g., “View receipt,” “Add to calendar”) presented as smaller, pill-shaped controls below. This keeps the right-hand side visually dominant but less demanding, indicating completion while maintaining the structural rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same static right column card area as the registration form; it scrolls with the page and benefits from any sticky behavior applied to the registration card on larger viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there is a page-level event error (such as the event not loading or being unavailable), the error state appears directly below the breadcrumb navigation, effectively replacing the hero and main content areas. It is displayed as a full-width alert band within the central content container, with a contrasting background (e.g., soft red or amber) spanning the full content width and 24–32px of vertical padding. The error message is center-aligned or left-aligned within this band, using a short, bold title (e.g., “Event unavailable”) followed by one or two concise lines of explanatory text. There is 24–32px of whitespace above and below the band, and no hero or registration card is shown beneath it in severe error cases, making the issue immediately clear. For more localized errors (e.g., registration failures), a smaller error strip appears near the top of the right-hand registration card, aligned left within the card and spanning its width, sitting 16–24px above the first relevant field.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: For global event errors, the alert band is a static block under the breadcrumb that scrolls normally and replaces the hero/registration content area; for localized registration errors, the alert is anchored within the right-hand card and scrolls with it, occupying the top portion of that card.

name: Membership Plans & Pricing
title: Membership Plans & Pricing
description: This page lets users view, compare, and select membership plans and terms, configure auto-renewal, and proceed to checkout with the chosen options.

core_features: Load and display all active membership plans and their available terms using getMembershipPlans.
Highlight a specific membership plan when the page is opened with a highlight_plan URL parameter.
Allow users to select a membership plan and one of its available terms.
Provide a Compare view/tab that shows key benefits and differences between multiple membership plans.
Fetch detailed information for a selected plan on demand using getMembershipPlanDetails (for comparison rows or detail views).
Allow users to toggle Auto-Renew on or off for the selected membership term.
Enable a primary action (e.g., Continue / Join Now) that navigates to membership_checkout.html, passing membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew via URL parameters.
user_workflows: Browse and select a plan and term: (1) User navigates to membership_plans.html. (2) Page calls getMembershipPlans and displays a list of active plans with their term options. (3) User reviews plans in the List view. (4) User selects a plan card, which expands or focuses that plan and shows its available terms. (5) User selects one term for that plan. (6) User optionally toggles Auto-Renew on or off. (7) User clicks Continue / Join Now. (8) Page navigates to membership_checkout.html with membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew encoded in the URL.

Use Compare view to choose a plan: (1) User opens membership_plans.html and sees List and Compare view controls. (2) User switches to Compare view. (3) Page uses the previously loaded plans from getMembershipPlans to populate a comparison selector and basic comparison table. (4) When a plan is added to the comparison, the page may call getMembershipPlanDetails for that plan to enrich the comparison table (benefits, additional attributes). (5) User compares included benefits and term pricing across selected plans. (6) From the Compare table, user clicks Choose Plan on one of the compared plans. (7) The main selection state is updated to that plan, the user is shown its terms, and a term is selected (either default or by the user). (8) User sets Auto-Renew preference and clicks Continue / Join Now to go to checkout.

Deep-link from promotion with highlighted plan: (1) User clicks a membership link from index.html or about.html that includes a highlight_plan URL parameter (e.g., membership_plans.html?highlight_plan=some_plan_code). (2) On page load, membership_plans.html reads the highlight_plan query parameter. (3) Page calls getMembershipPlans. (4) When plans are loaded, the plan whose code matches highlight_plan is programmatically highlighted (e.g., scrolled into view, visually marked, and/or preselected). (5) The highlighted plan’s default term (if defined) is preselected. (6) User optionally changes term or Auto-Renew, then clicks Continue / Join Now and is navigated to membership_checkout.html with the selected plan, term, and autoRenew in the URL.

Refine selection and shareable state via URL: (1) User opens membership_plans.html without parameters. (2) User selects a specific plan, term, view mode (List or Compare), and Auto-Renew setting. (3) The page updates the browser URL query parameters to reflect current state (e.g., selected plan ID, selected term ID, view mode, autoRenew). (4) User copies the URL and shares it or bookmarks it. (5) Another user (or the original user later) opens the same URL. (6) On load, the page reads these URL parameters, restores the same plan selection, term, view, and autoRenew value, and then the user can immediately proceed to checkout if desired.
interactions: On page load, membership_plans.html calls getMembershipPlans with no parameters and binds the returned plans to the plans list and comparison features.
When the page detects a highlight_plan URL parameter after loading plans, it matches this value against each plan.code and sets the matching plan as highlighted and optionally preselected.
When a user clicks on a plan card in the list, the selectedPlan state is updated, the plan card is expanded or focused, and the term selector for that plan becomes active.
When a user selects a term option within a plan, the selectedTerm state is updated, and the URL is updated with a term-related parameter so that the selection can be restored on reload.
When a user toggles Auto-Renew on or off, the autoRenew state is updated and the URL is updated with an autoRenew parameter (e.g., autoRenew=true/false).
When a user switches between List view and Compare view, the current view mode is stored in state and mirrored into a view URL parameter (e.g., view=list or view=compare).
When a user adds a plan to the comparison set in Compare view, the plan ID is added to an in-memory comparison list and reflected in a URL parameter (e.g., compare=planId1,planId2).

When a plan is included in the comparison set and additional details are needed, the page calls getMembershipPlanDetails with that plan’s id to populate additional comparison fields (e.g., benefit lists).
When a user clicks a Choose Plan button from within the comparison table, selectedPlan is updated to that plan, and the list view (or a detail area) shows its terms for selection.

When the user clicks Continue / Join Now, the page validates that selectedPlan, selectedTerm, and autoRenew are set, then navigates to membership_checkout.html, appending membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew as URL query parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state is controlled and synchronized using URL parameters so that the page can restore selections on reload and support deep-linking. The highlight_plan parameter is read on initial load; after getMembershipPlans returns, the page finds the plan with a matching code and sets it as highlighted/selected. Additional internal parameters may be used: (1) view: indicates current view mode (e.g., list or compare) and is updated whenever the user toggles the view; on load, if view is present, the page initializes the UI to that mode. (2) selected_plan_id: stores the ID of the currently selected plan; when a user selects a plan card or chooses a plan from Compare view, this parameter is updated; on load, if present, the page selects the matching plan once plans are loaded. (3) selected_term_id: stores the ID of the currently selected term within the selected plan; when the user changes term, this parameter is updated; on load, if present and the selected plan has a matching term, that term is auto-selected. (4) autoRenew: stores the current Auto-Renew preference as a boolean-like string (e.g., true or false); when the toggle is changed, it updates this parameter; on load, if present, the auto-renew toggle initializes from this value, otherwise defaults from system rules. (5) compare: stores a comma-separated list of plan IDs currently in the comparison set; when a plan is added or removed from the comparison, this parameter is updated; on load, after plans are fetched, the page parses this parameter and rebuilds the comparison set. The only parameters passed to membership_checkout.html are membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew, which are populated from the current selectedPlan.id, selectedTerm.id, and the current autoRenew state at the time the user initiates checkout.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and global state for membership plans, selected plan and term, view mode, comparison set, and auto-renew preference.
Fetches all active membership plans and their available terms via getMembershipPlans and exposes them to other components.
Allows users to switch between List view and Compare view for membership plans and updates the view URL parameter to keep the mode shareable.

Displays all active membership plans (from getMembershipPlans) in a list layout, highlighting the plan referenced by highlight_plan or selected_plan_id, and exposing per-plan actions such as select, view details, and add to comparison.

Represents a single membership plan within the list, showing its name, base pricing information from availableTerms, a subset of benefits if supplied in the plan object, and controls for selecting the plan, opening term selection, and adding/removing from comparison.
For the currently selected plan, displays that plan’s availableTerms array and allows the user to select one term; updates selectedTerm state and URL selected_term_id parameter when a term is chosen.
Provides a switch for enabling or disabling auto-renewal for the currently selected membership term; updates autoRenew state and the autoRenew URL parameter on change.

Shows detailed information for the currently selected plan, including its full description and benefits. If the plan displayed needs more detail than provided by getMembershipPlans, this component requests additional data via getMembershipPlanDetails and merges it into the view.

Allows users to add or remove plans from the comparison set, keeps the list of compared plan IDs in sync with the compare URL parameter, and triggers loading of extra details with getMembershipPlanDetails when needed for comparison.

Displays selected plans side-by-side in Compare view using data from getMembershipPlans and any additional details from getMembershipPlanDetails (e.g., benefits). Provides Choose Plan actions to set the main selection to a chosen plan.

Reads the highlight_plan URL parameter on load and, after plans are available, finds and marks the matching plan (by code) as highlighted and optionally selected, updating selected_plan_id in the URL to lock in this state.

Keeps the selected plan ID, selected term ID, view mode, compare set, and autoRenew preference synchronized between in-memory state and the URL query parameters, ensuring that reloading or sharing the URL restores the current selection.

Displays the primary Continue / Join Now action once a valid plan and term are selected and an autoRenew value is set. When activated, constructs the membership_checkout.html URL with membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew query parameters populated from current state and performs the navigation.

When any component requests more information for a specific plan (e.g., from comparison or detail views), calls getMembershipPlanDetails with membershipPlanId and provides the returned plan object to the requesting components, enhancing existing plan data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. Header content (logo, nav, search) is aligned along this grid with clear left, center, and right zones.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear top-to-bottom structure echoing the mockup: a global fixed header at the very top (logo left, nav center-left, search right), then a full-bleed photographic hero for Membership Plans & Pricing with a centered headline and primary CTA. Below the hero, the main pricing interface sits in a centered content container. Within this container, a horizontal control strip (view mode, term selector, auto-renew) sits at the top, and the main content area below switches between list view (plan cards plus details panel) and compare view (comparison selector plus comparison table). A sticky checkout bar runs along the bottom edge of the viewport once a plan is selected.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear top-to-bottom structure echoing the mockup: a global fixed header at the very top (logo left, nav center-left, search right), then a full-bleed photographic hero for Membership Plans & Pricing with a centered headline and primary CTA. Below the hero, the main pricing interface sits in a centered content container. Within this container, a horizontal control strip (view mode, term selector, auto-renew) sits at the top, and the main content area below switches between list view (plan cards plus details panel) and compare view (comparison selector plus comparison table). A sticky checkout bar runs along the bottom edge of the viewport once a plan is selected.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header is relatively shallow, around 15–20% of the initial viewport height. The hero occupies the remaining 80–85% of the first screen, with wide left/right breathing room around a centered text block. The pricing interface beneath the hero uses a max-width content container aligned to a 12-column grid. The top control strip consumes roughly 15–20% of the vertical space in the pricing area, with the remaining 80–85% used by either the plan cards plus a right-hand details panel (desktop) or a full-width comparison table. The sticky checkout bar occupies a slim band at the bottom (about 10–12% of viewport height) and sits above page content. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero to controls, controls to content blocks) are in the 48–64px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious: each major band (hero, controls, plans, comparison) has clear top and bottom padding. Controls are grouped into logical clusters with consistent horizontal spacing, leaving whitespace around them so nothing feels cramped. Plan cards have generous internal padding and clear separation between cards. The comparison table is structured and scannable, with enough row height and column spacing to avoid visual overload despite more data density. The sticky checkout bar is compact but not crowded, with clear hierarchy between summary text and the primary CTA button.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top-left logo, moving across the header navigation to the search in a classic F-pattern. The hero then draws attention to the centered Membership headline and the primary pill-shaped CTA, anchoring focus around the middle of the screen. As users scroll, the eye lands on the centered control strip, scanning left (view mode) to right (auto-renew and compare selector), then drops into the plan cards or comparison table. Within list view, the eye moves horizontally across cards, then vertically into the details panel on the right when a plan is selected. The sticky checkout bar at the very bottom completes the flow, providing a persistent, high-contrast path to proceed to checkout once selections are made.

layout.overall_description: Below a fixed, full-width header and a dramatic full-bleed climbing hero, the Membership Plans & Pricing interface sits in a centered content band that feels like a structured, professional application layered onto a marketing site. A wide control strip just below the hero lets visitors toggle between list and compare views, choose billing terms, and set auto-renew, all with pill-shaped toggles and buttons echoing the hero CTA. In list view, a row of clean, evenly spaced membership cards spans most of the page width, with a dedicated details panel on the right that deepens information about the currently highlighted plan without overwhelming the main grid. In compare view, the layout shifts to a full-width comparison table where each plan occupies a column, preserving generous row height and column spacing for readability. Throughout, whitespace between sections and components preserves a sense of calm. As soon as a plan and term are selected, a slim, sticky checkout navigation bar slides into view at the bottom of the screen, summarizing the chosen membership and presenting a bold pill-shaped Proceed to Checkout button, aligning the entire experience toward confident conversion while staying consistent with the clean, modern visual language from the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual page controller is conceptually anchored to the very top of the main content area, immediately below the global header and hero. It occupies no visible space but wraps the entire Membership Plans & Pricing page logic, orchestrating state across the view mode, plans list, comparison table, and checkout bar. Spatially, it can be considered the root container for all other membership-related components within the central content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, full-page controller attached to the main content root; occupies no layout space and does not affect visual flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans data loader sits logically near the top of the main pricing section, just inside the central content container that begins below the hero. While invisible, it underpins the first visible band of controls and content. If loading states are represented, they appear as a centered, unobtrusive loading indicator occupying the same horizontal span as the upcoming controls, with vertical padding of roughly 48px above and below to preserve the spacious look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual in its normal state; any transient loading indicator is centered horizontally within the content container and vertically padded, then removed once data has loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view mode toggle is placed at the left side of the top control strip that appears immediately below the hero, inside the centered content container. It aligns to the left grid edge and sits about 48px beneath the bottom of the hero content. The toggle appears as a pill-shaped segmented control (for example, List vs Compare) spanning about 20–25% of the content width on desktop. There is at least 16–24px of horizontal breathing room between it and neighboring controls, and 16–24px of vertical padding within the control itself. This position makes it the first interactive element encountered when scanning left-to-right in the pricing controls area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the top control strip; it maintains its left-aligned position as the page scrolls, and on narrower screens it shifts to a centered block with full-width pill styling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans list occupies the main left and central portion of the content area in list view, directly below the control strip and aligned to the central grid. There is a vertical gap of approximately 32–48px between the controls and the top of the first row of plan cards, reinforcing section separation. On desktop, the list arranges plan-card components in a responsive grid of two to three cards per row, each card spanning roughly 3–4 columns of the 12-column grid, with 24–32px gutters between cards and 24–32px vertical spacing between rows. The list itself extends downward, taking up the majority of the viewport height below the controls, while leaving a consistent right-side margin to accommodate the optional plan details panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content within the main page flow; in list view it fills the majority of the main content area, and in compare view it is fully hidden or collapsed to preserve focus on the comparison table.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each plan card appears as a vertically oriented card within the plans list grid. On desktop, a single card occupies roughly 30–33% of the content width (3–4 grid columns) with generous internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. Cards have equal height where possible, with 24–32px gaps between cards horizontally and vertically, preserving the spacious aesthetic. Title and price sit near the top, benefits in the middle, and a secondary choose or details action at the bottom, creating a simple top-to-bottom information hierarchy. The most recommended or highlighted plan (as driven by URL or state) can visually extend slightly taller, or use a stronger border or background tint, but still respects the same grid footprint so the grid remains clean and aligned.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the grid; cards reflow responsively from three-per-row on wide screens to two-per-row on medium screens, then to full-width stacked cards on small screens, always maintaining consistent gutters and internal padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan term selector is placed in the same top control strip as the view mode toggle, but visually centered horizontally within the content container to reflect its global importance. It appears as a pill-shaped segmented control (for example, Monthly, 6 Months, Annual) spanning approximately 30–40% of the content width. It sits on the same horizontal line as the view mode toggle and auto-renew toggle, with 16–24px horizontal spacing from each neighboring element. Vertically, it shares the same 48px band as the other controls, with enough surrounding whitespace that each segment is easy to tap and read. On narrower screens, the term selector moves to a dedicated row just beneath the view mode toggle, centered and full width with consistent 16–24px vertical separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control strip; it remains near the top of the pricing area as the user scrolls. On very small screens, it can become horizontally scrollable if the number of terms is large while maintaining pill styling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auto-renew toggle is positioned on the far right of the top control strip, aligned to the right edge of the central content container. It shares a horizontal baseline with the view mode toggle and term selector, creating a balanced left-to-right bar of controls. The auto-renew control presents as a pill-shaped toggle or switch with a concise label, occupying roughly 15–20% of the content width. There is at least 16px of padding between the toggle and the right container edge. This location means that when scanning the control strip, users naturally end at the auto-renew option before moving down into the plans themselves.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the control strip; on smaller screens, it drops to a second or third row of controls beneath the term selector, right-aligned or centered depending on space, but always maintaining comfortable vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan details panel sits to the right of the plans list in list view, forming a two-column layout within the central content container on desktop. It occupies roughly 3–4 columns (about 25–33% of the width), anchored to the right grid edge, with a 24–32px horizontal gutter separating it from the nearest plan cards. The panel stretches vertically to match or slightly exceed the height of the visible row of cards, with internal padding of 24–32px and clear typographic hierarchy for features, fine print, and any legal notes. When no plan is selected, the panel can display a subtle placeholder or recommended plan summary with subdued styling. On tablets and smaller devices, the panel shifts below the plans list as a full-width block with 48px of vertical separation from the last row of cards, maintaining the same internal padding and card-like appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content container, but responsive: right-hand column on larger screens and full-width section beneath the plans list on smaller screens. It can optionally become sticky within its column while scrolling the list if content height is shorter than the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison selector appears directly beneath the top control strip, aligned with the left edge of the content container, but only when the view mode is set to Compare (or when compare functionality is active). It sits in a narrow horizontal band with about 16–24px vertical padding above and below, separated from the control strip by a 16–24px gap to keep the hierarchy clear. The selector elements (for example, checkboxes or pill chips representing plans to include in the comparison) may span 50–70% of the width, wrapped across one or two lines as needed, maintaining at least 8–16px spacing between chips. On the right side of this band, there is room for a small helper text or reset link, aligned to the right edge. This band leads directly down into the comparison table below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static band at the top of the compare view; when users switch away from compare mode, this selector collapses or is hidden entirely, freeing vertical space and keeping the list view cleaner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison table occupies nearly the full width of the central content container directly beneath the comparison selector, replacing the plan cards in compare view. It starts approximately 24–32px below the comparison selector and extends downward, potentially beyond the viewport. Columns represent plans, and rows represent features, with the header row pinned visually at the top of the table area. The table uses a generous row height (at least 40–56px) and 24–32px horizontal cell padding to maintain readability. On desktop, the table spans about 90–100% of the content width with subtle left and right padding to keep it aligned to the grid. On narrow screens, columns can become horizontally scrollable within a full-width container, while the header row remains fixed at the top of that scrolling area to preserve context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow but can introduce an internal horizontal scroll on small screens. In list view, the table is hidden; in compare view, it becomes the dominant central element, pushing the details panel out of view or collapsing it below the table if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This URL-state handler has no visible UI but conceptually overlays the plan cards and details panel. It ensures that if a plan is specified in the URL, that card appears visually highlighted within the plans list (for example, with a stronger border, background tint, or badge) and its information is loaded into the plan details panel. Spatially, you can think of it as governing state within the grid area where plan cards and the details panel reside, rather than occupying separate space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it reacts to URL changes to alter styles and content in existing visible components without adding any new layout elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This URL-state sync component quietly connects user interactions in the plans list, details panel, and compare table to the browser URL, with no direct visual representation. It operates at the page level, in parallel with the plans-page-init controller, influencing the state of highlighted plans, selected terms, and compare choices. It does not introduce any visual element or affect spacing but ensures that the current layout state can be restored from the URL.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, full-page behavior that updates URL parameters in response to user actions; no spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout navigation bar appears as a full-width, pill-accented band anchored to the bottom edge of the viewport, above any browser UI, once a user has selected a valid plan and term. It spans 100% of the window width but is visually constrained by the same central content width, so its inner content aligns with the main grid. The bar has a height of roughly 64–80px, with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding on both sides. Inside, a concise summary of the selected plan sits on the left, while a bold pill-shaped primary button (for example, Proceed to Checkout) sits on the right, aligned with the header and content container. The background of the bar has high contrast against the page content, and a subtle drop shadow separates it from underlying content. There is at least 80–96px of bottom margin in the main page content to ensure no important information is hidden behind the bar when it is visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport; it slides in once conditions are met and stays visible as the user scrolls through plans, providing a persistent primary action. On mobile, it may compress to a slightly taller bar with stacked text and button, still spanning full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan details fetcher is logically attached to the plan details panel and any deep-dive information triggered from plan cards, but it has no direct visual form. It ensures that when a user selects a plan, the right-hand details panel (or the full-width details section on smaller screens) is populated with the most current and complete information. Spatially, it can be thought of as living inside the same grid column as the details panel, governing its content without adding any additional layout elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual data-loading behavior; it does not occupy space but may temporarily trigger a lightweight loading state within the existing bounds of the plan details panel.

name: Membership Checkout
title: Membership Checkout
description: Review the selected membership plan and term, enter billing and payment details, and complete the membership purchase.

core_features: Initialize checkout by reading membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew from the URL and calling getMembershipCheckoutDetails to retrieve the plan, selected term, and total due.
Display a dynamic checkout summary including membership plan information, selected term details, price, currency, auto-renew status, and total amount due.
Allow users to adjust the auto-renew setting on the checkout page, updating the URL parameter and refreshing the checkout summary via getMembershipCheckoutDetails.
Collect required purchaser information, including full name, email address, and billing address fields (address line 1, optional address line 2, city, state, postal code, country).
Collect payment details for Credit/Debit Card, including card number, expiration date, and CVV, and prepare them for secure submission.
Submit the membership purchase using purchaseMembership with URL-derived plan/term/autoRenew plus form-derived billing and payment data.
Handle purchase outcomes by displaying success or error messages, showing a purchase confirmation summary, and providing a 'Return to Home' navigation link to index.html after a successful purchase.
Manage loading, validation, and error states for both the checkout details retrieval and the purchase submission, disabling interactive elements as needed.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Landing on the Membership Checkout page
1. User arrives on membership_checkout.html via a link that includes membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew as URL query parameters.
2. On page load, the page parses these URL parameters.
3. If any required parameter is missing or invalid, the page displays an error message and disables the checkout form, offering a link back to membership_plans.html.
4. If parameters are valid, the page calls getMembershipCheckoutDetails with membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew.
5. While waiting for the response, a loading state is shown for the summary and the form is disabled.
6. On success, the checkout summary is populated and the form becomes enabled.
7. On failure (e.g., invalid term for plan, inactive plan), an error message is shown and the form remains disabled.

Workflow: Adjusting Auto-Renew on the Checkout Page
1. After the summary loads, the user sees the current auto-renew status that was passed in via the URL.
2. The user toggles the auto-renew control in the summary component.
3. The page updates the autoRenew URL parameter (for example, using history.replaceState to avoid a full reload) with the new value.
4. The page calls getMembershipCheckoutDetails again with the same membershipPlanId and termId but the updated autoRenew value.
5. While reloading, the summary displays a loading state for pricing/total due; the form remains available but the purchase button is temporarily disabled until new totals are loaded.
6. Once the updated details are returned, the summary (including totalDue and auto-renew label) is refreshed, and the purchase button is re-enabled.

Workflow: Completing the Membership Purchase
1. With a valid summary displayed, the user fills in required purchaser information: full name, email, and billing address fields.
2. The user selects the payment method (Credit/Debit Card) if not already preselected and enters card number, card expiration, and card CVV in the card section.
3. On clicking the 'Complete Purchase' button, the form runs client-side validation to ensure all required fields are filled and that a card number, expiration, and CVV are present for the selected payment method.
4. If validation fails, inline error messages are displayed and the API call is not made.
5. If validation passes, the page sets a 'purchase in progress' state, disables the form controls and purchase button, and calls purchaseMembership with: membershipPlanId, termId, autoRenew (from the URL/state); fullName, email, billing address fields (from the form); paymentMethod (credit_debit_card); and cardNumber, cardExpiration, cardCvv (from the card section).
6. While awaiting the purchaseMembership response, a loading indicator is shown near the purchase button or in a status area.
7. On a successful response (success = true), the page stores the returned membershipPurchase object in page state, sets a 'purchase success' flag, and updates the UI to show the confirmation panel. The checkout form is hidden or locked to prevent duplicate submissions.
8. On a failed response (success = false or paymentStatus indicating failure), an error message is shown, relevant fields may be highlighted, and the form and purchase button are re-enabled so the user can correct information and resubmit.

Workflow: Viewing Confirmation and Returning Home
1. After a successful purchase, the confirmation panel displays a success message and key details from membershipPurchase (such as planName, termLabel, price, currency, purchaserEmail, autoRenew, and masked card details like cardLast4 and cardExpiration when available).
2. The user reviews the confirmation details and sees note that an email receipt has been sent to purchaserEmail, if provided by the data.
3. The user can click a 'Return to Home' button.
4. This button navigates the user to index.html via a standard link, without carrying over sensitive purchase information in the URL.
5. If the user revisits membership_checkout.html later using the original URL (without a new purchase), the page behaves as a fresh checkout and does not attempt to re-create the past purchase, since there is no interface to retrieve an existing MembershipPurchase by id.
interactions: On page load, the script reads the membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew query parameters from the URL and triggers getMembershipCheckoutDetails; any change to these parameters via internal navigation logic can re-trigger this call.

When the auto-renew toggle is clicked in the summary, it updates the autoRenew state and URL parameter, then calls getMembershipCheckoutDetails again with the new value to recalculate totalDue; the UI reflects a temporary loading state for pricing.
When the user types into text input fields (full name, email, billing address), the form component updates its internal state and clears any related validation errors for that field.

When the user changes the payment method selection, the form updates its paymentMethod state; the card details section is shown or hidden depending on whether credit_debit_card is selected, and validation rules adapt accordingly.

When the user types card details (card number, expiration, CVV), the card section updates its internal state, may apply formatting/masking, and exposes card validity status back to the parent form component for overall validation.

On 'Complete Purchase' button click, the checkout form component performs client-side validation; on success, it invokes purchaseMembership and sets a 'purchase in progress' state, disabling form inputs and the button until a response is received.

On a successful purchaseMembership response, the page updates state with the membershipPurchase data, switches the view from the editable form to a read-only confirmation panel, and shows a success message.

On an error from purchaseMembership (network error or API-level failure), the page displays an error message in the status component, re-enables the form, and focuses or highlights fields if appropriate (for example, if the error indicates invalid card details).
On clicking 'Return to Home' from the confirmation panel, the browser navigates to index.html, ending the checkout flow.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is primarily driven by URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page parses membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew from the query string (for example: membership_checkout.html?membershipPlanId=...&termId=...&autoRenew=...). If membershipPlanId or termId are missing or empty, the page does not call getMembershipCheckoutDetails and instead enters an 'invalid parameters' state, showing an error and disabling the checkout form. If autoRenew is missing, the page may choose a default boolean value derived from business rules and treat that as the current auto-renew state, but it should still remain consistent with the URL once set.

When valid parameters are present, the page enters a 'checkout details loading' state and calls getMembershipCheckoutDetails(membershipPlanId, termId, autoRenew). While this call is pending, summary content shows a loading indicator and the form's purchase button is disabled to prevent premature submission. On success, the resulting plan (MembershipPlan), selectedTerm, autoRenew boolean, totalDue, and currency are stored in page state. An 'checkout details loaded' state flag is set, enabling the form for interaction.

If getMembershipCheckoutDetails returns an error (e.g., invalid combination, plan inactive), the page enters a 'checkout details error' state, displays the returned message (if available), and disables the form and purchase button. The URL is not changed in this case so that the user can correct the parameters manually or navigate back.

The auto-renew setting is treated as both a view state and URL-driven state. The current autoRenew value is always synchronized with the URL parameter: when the user toggles it in the UI, the page sets internal autoRenew state and updates the autoRenew query parameter (for example via history.replaceState), then re-calls getMembershipCheckoutDetails with the new autoRenew value. This ensures that the visible totalDue and the value passed later to purchaseMembership stay in sync and are reproducible if the user shares the URL during the checkout (before completing the purchase).

For the purchase flow, additional transient states are used: 'purchase idle', 'purchase in progress', 'purchase success', and 'purchase error'. When the user submits the form successfully (after client-side validation), the state moves to 'purchase in progress', and the page calls purchaseMembership with the current URL-driven membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew plus all billing and payment fields from the form. While in this state, inputs and the purchase button are disabled to prevent duplicate submissions. On response, if success is true and a MembershipPurchase object is returned, the page stores membershipPurchase in state, sets 'purchase success', and may optionally add a non-sensitive URL flag (such as a generic status parameter) purely for navigational purposes; however, it does not expose raw card data in the URL. If the response indicates failure, the page sets 'purchase error', shows an error message, and re-enables the form.

The state of whether to show the checkout form or the confirmation panel is derived from the 'purchase success' flag: before a successful purchase, the form and summary are visible and editable; after success, the form becomes read-only or hidden, and the confirmation panel is displayed using the membershipPurchase and plan data already stored in memory. No sensitive data is persisted beyond what is returned in membershipPurchase (e.g., cardLast4 and cardExpiration), and there is no dependence on localStorage; all state is maintained in memory during the page lifecycle and driven by URL parameters and live API responses.

components.functionality: Controls initial page setup and orchestrates data loading for the checkout. On page load, it parses membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew from the URL, validates them, and, when valid, calls getMembershipCheckoutDetails. It stores the resulting plan, selectedTerm, autoRenew, totalDue, and currency in shared page state, sets loading and error flags, and passes this state down to the checkout summary and form components. It also responds to changes in the autoRenew setting by updating the URL and re-invoking getMembershipCheckoutDetails.

Displays a read-only summary of the membership being purchased using data from getMembershipCheckoutDetails: plan name and category information, selected term label and duration, price, currency, auto-renew status, and totalDue. It shows loading and error states as indicated by the controller and disables interactive elements when details are not yet loaded. It includes an auto-renew toggle that the user can change, and when changed, it informs the controller to update the autoRenew URL parameter and reload pricing via getMembershipCheckoutDetails.

Collects purchaser and billing information and coordinates the purchase submission. It renders input fields for purchaserFullName, purchaserEmail, billingAddressLine1, optional billingAddressLine2, billingCity, billingState, billingPostalCode, and billingCountry. It also renders a payment method selector (focused on credit_debit_card) and hosts the card details section. The form validates required fields on input and on submission. On successful validation, it calls purchaseMembership with membershipPlanId, termId, and autoRenew from the controller plus all collected form and card fields, and manages 'purchase in progress', 'success', and 'error' states.

Handles Credit/Debit Card input fields within the checkout form. It collects cardNumber, cardExpiration, and cardCvv when the payment method is credit_debit_card. It may format and mask inputs (e.g., grouping digits or masking CVV in the UI) and exposes validation status back to the parent form. On form submission, it supplies cardNumber, cardExpiration, and cardCvv to membership-checkout-form so they can be passed to purchaseMembership. It does not store or expose full card details beyond the scope of the current submission and relies on the API response (membershipPurchase.cardLast4 and membershipPurchase.cardExpiration) for any stored display of card information.

Displays status and error messages for both checkout details loading and purchase submission. It listens to flags from the controller and form (e.g., 'checkout details loading', 'checkout details error', 'purchase in progress', 'purchase error') and renders appropriate messages or indicators. It may show generic errors when URL parameters are missing or invalid, and specific messages returned from purchaseMembership when available. It also visually indicates when actions are disabled due to ongoing API calls.

Shows after a successful purchaseMembership call. It uses membershipPurchase, along with plan information, to display confirmation details such as the planName, termLabel, price and currency, autoRenew status, purchaserEmail, and non-sensitive card information (cardLast4 and cardExpiration) if available. It hides or disables the editable form to prevent duplicate purchases. It also provides a 'Return to Home' button that links to index.html, implementing the outgoing navigation described for this page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. Header content (logo, nav, search) is aligned along this grid with clear left, center, and right zones.
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s header + hero pattern: a full-width fixed header at the very top (logo left, nav center, search right), then a full-bleed hero-like checkout area beneath it. Inside this hero, a centered content container runs on a 12-column grid. The checkout experience is presented as a prominent, card-like block sitting in the center of the hero: on larger screens, the left ~8 columns are devoted to the multi-step checkout form (including the payment card section), while the right ~4 columns hold a compact but visually strong membership summary. Status messages sit at the top of this card stack, and a confirmation panel later replaces the form+summary block entirely once purchase is complete.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s header + hero pattern: a full-width fixed header at the very top (logo left, nav center, search right), then a full-bleed hero-like checkout area beneath it. Inside this hero, a centered content container runs on a 12-column grid. The checkout experience is presented as a prominent, card-like block sitting in the center of the hero: on larger screens, the left ~8 columns are devoted to the multi-step checkout form (including the payment card section), while the right ~4 columns hold a compact but visually strong membership summary. Status messages sit at the top of this card stack, and a confirmation panel later replaces the form+summary block entirely once purchase is complete.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header keeps the same shallow 15–20% of initial viewport height. The hero/checkout area consumes the remaining 80–85%, with a full-bleed climbing background image subtly visible behind a high-contrast, slightly elevated white content card. Within the main card, vertical spacing between major segments (status messages, form sections, summary block, confirmation state) follows the 48–64px section spacing. Internal padding for the central card is 24–32px all around. On desktop, the left form column uses roughly 65–70% of the card width (8/12 columns) and the summary panel about 30–35% (4/12 columns). On mobile, the same content stacks vertically, preserving generous top and bottom padding between blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: The visual density mirrors the mockup: generous whitespace, large, legible type, and clear separation between sections. The form is broken into logical chunks (member info, billing address, card details) with 24–32px vertical spacing between groups and 16–24px between related fields. The summary panel is concise and visually punchy, using bold type for the plan name and price with minimal supporting details. Status messages are compact banners but not cramped, with 12–16px padding. The overall density emphasizes clarity and ease of completion over fitting in maximum information.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The viewing path repeats the mockup’s F/Z pattern adapted for checkout: the eye starts at the top-left logo, scans across the nav to the right-aligned search, then drops into the hero’s center where a bold page title/headline for checkout sits above the main card. From there, attention moves left to the start of the form, but is quickly balanced by the high-contrast summary panel on the right that confirms the chosen membership. The vertical flow runs down the form fields, ending at a large, pill-shaped primary button that echoes the red hero CTA. Status messages appear directly above the form to be noticed immediately when errors or status changes occur. After completion, the confirmation panel becomes the new focal point at the center of the hero, with a clear headline and next steps occupying the primary attention zone.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed, full-width header, the Membership Checkout page opens into a full-bleed hero area showing a softened, out-of-focus indoor climbing scene. Centered within this background is a white, slightly elevated card-like container aligned to the same 12-column grid as the header. At the top of this container sits the checkout page title with a short supporting line, and just beneath it a full-width status area reserved for alerts. The card then splits into a left-dominant column for the form and a right, narrower column for the membership summary. The left side carries the visitor smoothly from basic member details through billing information to a clearly delineated card entry section, with each segment separated by ample vertical space and subtle section headings. The right side keeps a compact, visually strong recap of the membership plan, price, term, and key benefits, helping reassure the user they’ve selected the right option. At the bottom of the left column, a bold red, pill-shaped button anchors the call to action, mirroring the hero CTA style from the mockup. Once the purchase is successful, the form and summary collapse away and are replaced by a centered confirmation panel that occupies the same overall footprint: a large success icon, strong confirmation headline, and a succinct recap of the membership stand out against the background image, creating a focused, celebratory end state while preserving the same clean, modern, marketing-driven aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy explicit screen real estate. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the main content area inside the central hero container, above all other checkout components. It governs which combination of components is visible at any given time: initially, it shows the checkout-status-messages (if any), membership-checkout-form, payment-card-section, and checkout-summary-panel arranged in their two-column layout. After a successful transaction, it hides the form, payment, and summary areas and reveals the purchase-confirmation-panel in the same central zone. Spatially, think of it as an invisible layer just below the header and hero background that orchestrates which card content is rendered within the centered checkout card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical/controller; no rendered box. It influences the presence, stacking, and state of other components but does not itself affect layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout summary panel appears inside the main white card, on the right side, occupying roughly 4 of the 12 columns of the content grid (about 30–35% of the card width) on desktop. It is aligned with the top of the membership-checkout-form’s input area, starting immediately to the right of the form column with a 24–32px gap between the two. The panel runs from just below the page title/status area down to slightly above the bottom of the form, with internal padding of about 24–32px. The background is either pure white with a soft outline or a very light tinted block to distinguish it from the surrounding card, keeping a clean, card-within-card feel. The plan name and pricing are placed near the top-left of this panel, using large, bold type that mirrors the hierarchy of the hero headline but scaled down for a sidebar. Secondary details (term length, renewal info, key benefits) stack vertically with 8–16px spacing. On tall screens, the panel’s height roughly matches the main form area, but if the form extends further on scroll, the panel may be slightly shorter, retaining a balanced but not perfectly symmetrical look. On tablet and mobile widths, this panel drops below the form and stretches closer to full width of the content container (about 90–100%), with consistent 24px padding and 32–48px vertical separation from the form section above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content card, but on larger screens it can adopt a subtle sticky behavior: its top edge pins just below the page title/status area while the user scrolls the form, ensuring the summary remains visible within the viewport until the bottom of the checkout card is reached.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership checkout form occupies the primary left column inside the central hero card, using approximately 8 of the 12 grid columns (about 65–70% of the card width) on desktop. It begins just below the page title and any visible status messages, aligned flush with the left edge of the content container’s grid. A generous top margin of about 24–32px separates the title/status region from the first form heading. The form is vertically structured into clear sections: member details at the top, billing/contact information in the middle, and the payment-card-section embedded toward the bottom. Each section is separated by 24–32px of vertical space, with section headings in slightly larger, bold text and inputs stacked in a single or two-column arrangement depending on viewport width. Inputs have comfortable vertical spacing (16–24px between form rows) and horizontal gutters that align with the base 8px grid. At the very bottom of this form column, the primary ‘Complete Purchase’ pill-shaped button spans roughly 60–80% of the left column width, aligned left, with 16–20px vertical padding and 24–32px top margin separating it from the last input field. Below the primary button, secondary links (e.g., ‘Back’ or ‘Change plan’) appear in smaller text with 8–12px spacing. On tablet and mobile, this form becomes full-width within the container (approximately 90–100% of the viewport width bounded by the central content max-width), and fields that were side by side collapse into a single column while preserving the generous vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing vertically within the main content card. Its height defines the overall scroll length of the checkout area. The form column always appears directly above the fold on typical laptop viewports to keep the first fields immediately accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment card section is a visually distinct sub-block nested within the membership-checkout-form’s left column, positioned as the last major section before the primary action button. It spans the full width of the form column (all 8 columns on desktop) and is separated from the preceding billing/details section by 24–32px of vertical whitespace. The section is framed like a subtle inner card: a very light background tint or a faint border, with internal padding of about 24px on all sides, giving the sense of a focused, secure area. At the top-left of this block, a clear label such as ‘Payment Details’ is set in bold, followed by the card number, expiry, CVC, and postal code fields arranged logically. On desktop, card number may stretch nearly full width, while expiry and CVC sit side by side using half-width columns with 16–24px horizontal gutter between them, all aligned to the grid. Error messages related to card input appear immediately under their respective fields with minimal but readable spacing (8px top margin) to avoid increasing perceived complexity. At the bottom of this section, subtle card logos or security assurances can be aligned to the right, visually balancing the heading at the top-left. On narrower screens, all inputs stack vertically and the inner padding remains consistent, giving the section a compact but breathable feel. The primary CTA button sits 24–32px below this whole block, preserving its visual independence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a nested, static section within the form. It scrolls together with the form and aligns to the same left column grid; no independent floating or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status messages area is positioned at the top of the main white checkout card, directly beneath the page headline and any brief descriptive subline. It spans the full width of the content container inside the card (across both the left form and right summary space), creating a unified horizontal band. When active, each message appears as a pill-topped banner or solid bar with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, using brand colors (e.g., red for error, green or neutral for success/info) in a way that aligns with the red accents in the search icon and hero CTAs. Messages are left-aligned, with icons (warning, info) preceding the text, and are separated from the start of the form by 16–24px of whitespace. If multiple messages need to be shown, they stack vertically with 8–12px gaps, but generally only one is visible to stay consistent with the clean, low-density aesthetic. When no message is present, this area collapses, and the form shifts up, maintaining a clean top edge. On mobile, the status band still spans nearly the full viewport width within the central container, ensuring that any errors or processing states are immediately visible above the form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content card, occupying space in the normal flow only when active. It pushes the form and summary down when shown and collapses cleanly when dismissed or absent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful purchase, the confirmation panel replaces the entire two-column form+summary layout within the central hero card. It is centered within the content container and spans roughly 6–8 of the 12 available columns (about 50–70% of the container width), giving it a prominent but airy presence. The panel sits atop the same white, slightly elevated card background, with 32px internal padding on all sides and at least 48px of vertical whitespace separating it from the card’s top edge. Near the top center of this panel, a large success icon or subtle graphic (e.g., a checkmark in a circular red accent that matches the brand CTA color) anchors the eye, followed immediately by a bold, multi-line confirmation headline centered horizontally. Underneath, a concise summary of the membership purchased (plan name, term, start date) is presented in a left-aligned block within the panel, while optional next steps (e.g., ‘Manage account’, ‘View receipt’) are grouped below with 16–24px spacing. A primary button, echoing the red pill styling from the hero and checkout CTA, is placed centered or left-aligned near the bottom of the panel with 24–32px top margin, serving as the main next-step action. The rest of the background remains the soft, full-bleed climbing image, now with more breathing room around the central confirmation card. On mobile, the panel width expands to nearly the full width of the safe content area (about 90–100%), while maintaining the same vertical hierarchy and generous internal spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and centered within the hero’s content container. It appears only in the post-purchase state, occupying the same overall vertical space that the form and summary previously used and preventing any underlying checkout components from being visible or scrollable.

name: Articles Listing
title: Articles
description: Search, filter, and browse technical and educational articles, with results controlled by URL parameters for easy sharing and revisiting.

core_features: Keyword search of articles using the searchArticles interface, with the query synchronized to the URL parameter q.
Filtering by skill level and publication date range using searchArticles parameters skillLevel, startDate, and endDate, with skill level options loaded from getArticleFilterOptions.
Sorting articles by criteria returned from getArticleFilterOptions.sortOptions (e.g., most recent first), mapped to the URL parameter sort and the searchArticles sortBy argument.
