component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element at the top-left of the control band within the main content column; remains in normal document flow and shifts to full-width on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gym results list occupies the main content area beneath the control band, aligned to the same centered container as the summary and controls. It begins approximately 24–32px below the control band to maintain visual separation, and it spans about 70–80% of the viewport width on large screens (the full width of the content column). The list is a vertical stack of gym cards arranged in a single column, maintaining the marketing site’s linear storytelling feel rather than a multi-column grid of small tiles. Each card stretches the full width of the list container, separated from the next card by 24–32px of vertical whitespace. The background behind the list is a solid, neutral color (typically white), with the page margins on either side providing clear framing and breathing room. As users scroll, the list extends downward, becoming the primary scrollable content area, while the header and hero remain above. On smaller screens, the list remains a single column, but side margins reduce to 16–24px to maximize space for the cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertically scrolling list within the main content flow; no sticky or fixed behavior. Cards are stacked in a single column and expand vertically as content grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each gym card is a prominent, white (or very light) rectangular container inside the results list, designed to feel like a mini marketing panel for a specific gym. Horizontally, the card spans nearly the full width of the results list container: on a 12-column grid, it might occupy 10–12 columns with 1–2 columns of padding or margin on each side, depending on breakpoint. The card uses generous internal padding—24–32px on all sides—to align with the spacing system. The layout within the card is structured: at the top left, the gym name appears as a strong heading; directly below are key details like city/state and distance, followed by a row or block of pill-shaped tags for amenities (e.g., “Lead climbing,” “Youth programs”). On the right side of the card (on desktop), occupying roughly 3–4 grid columns, sits a secondary column holding a small photo thumbnail or brand-colored block and a right-aligned primary action button (e.g., a pill-shaped “View Details” or “See Gym”). This creates a left-to-right reading flow inside the card. On smaller screens, the card’s content stacks into a single column: heading, details, tags, and then the action button full-width at the bottom. Each card is separated from the next by 24–32px of vertical space, with a subtle shadow or border to distinguish it from the background, echoing the clean, modern marketing aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the results list, each card is a standard block-level element that stretches horizontally within the content column and grows vertically based on its content. It reflows from a two-column internal layout on larger screens to a single-column stack on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notice appears as a full-width alert bar within the main content column, positioned directly below the hero section and above the control band. It aligns with the same centered container as the summary and list, spanning the full column width (about 70–80% of the viewport on large screens). The notice uses a high-contrast style consistent with the brand—likely a light background with a red-accent border or icon—to stand out while still fitting the clean, corporate aesthetic. There is 24–32px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the hero and the top of the error notice, and another 16–24px between the bottom of the error notice and the results summary/control band. The text within the notice is left-aligned, with concise messaging and possibly a link or “Try again” action button, padded by 16–24px on all sides. When no error is present, this space collapses, allowing the control band to move up directly under the hero, keeping the layout tight and efficient.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the vertical content flow that appears conditionally; it pushes the control band and results list downward when visible and collapses entirely when not needed.

name: Gym Detail
title: Gym Details
description: Displays detailed information for a selected gym, allows users to save the gym to their Favorite Gyms list, and provides navigation to nearby gyms and back to the gym directory or search results.

core_features: Load and display details for a single gym based on the gymId URL parameter using the getGymDetails interface, including description, location, and programs.
Show gym contact information (address, phone, email, website) using Gym entity fields returned by getGymDetails.
Display available gym programs and surface whether youth programs are offered based on hasYouthPrograms and programs fields.
Allow users to add the currently viewed gym to their Favorite Gyms list via an action button that calls addGymToFavorites and reflects the favorite state using the isFavorite flag from getGymDetails.
Optionally display a list of nearby or related gyms using the nearbyGyms array from getGymDetails, with each gym linking to its own Gym Details page via gymId in the URL.
Provide navigation back to the gym directory or search results (gyms.html) via a Back to Results or Back to Gym Directory link that preserves relevant URL parameters when available.
Optionally allow users to view their Favorite Gyms list from this page by calling getFavoriteGyms and displaying the returned gyms for quick navigation to other saved gyms.
user_workflows: View gym details: 1) User arrives on gym_detail.html with a gymId parameter in the URL. 2) On page load, the page reads the gymId from the URL. 3) The page calls getGymDetails(gymId). 4) When the response returns, the main gym details (Gym entity), favorite status (isFavorite), and nearbyGyms list are stored in page state. 5) Components render the gym header, contact information, program list, favorite button state, and nearby gyms list based on this data.

Mark a gym as favorite: 1) User views a gym on gym_detail.html and sees the favorite action button reflecting the current isFavorite state from getGymDetails. 2) If the gym is not already in the favorites list, the button is enabled to add the gym as a favorite. 3) User clicks the favorite button, triggering addGymToFavorites(gymId). 4) While the request is in progress, the button shows a loading or disabled state. 5) On successful response (success=true), the page updates its local isFavorite state and can optionally refresh or reconcile with the returned favoriteGymsList. 6) A success message is shown. 7) On failure (success=false), an error message is shown and the favorite state is not changed.

View the Favorite Gyms list from the gym detail page (optional): 1) User clicks a control such as a link or button to view Favorite Gyms. 2) The page calls getFavoriteGyms() on demand. 3) When the response returns, the page stores favoriteGymsList and the associated gyms array in state. 4) A favorites summary panel or overlay displays the list of Gym entities returned by getFavoriteGyms. 5) User clicks any gym in this list to navigate to its details, which updates the URL to gym_detail.html?gymId={selectedGymId} and triggers the standard detail loading flow via getGymDetails.

Navigate to a nearby or related gym: 1) User scrolls to the Nearby Gyms section populated from getGymDetails.nearbyGyms. 2) Each nearby gym entry is rendered as a link or clickable item that points to gym_detail.html with that gym's id in the gymId URL parameter. 3) When the user clicks a nearby gym, the browser navigates to the new URL. 4) The page reads the updated gymId and calls getGymDetails for the new gym, updating all components accordingly.

Return to the gym directory or previous search results: 1) User clicks the Back to Results or Back to Gym Directory link. 2) The link target is gyms.html, optionally including any preserved query parameters from the current URL (such as search filters or origin markers) so that the results page can reconstruct the prior search context. 3) The browser navigates to gyms.html with those parameters. 4) The gym_detail.html page does not maintain separate client-side history; navigation behavior is driven purely by URL changes.

Refresh favorite state when viewing a gym: 1) On initial load via getGymDetails, the page reads isFavorite from the response and uses it to set the initial favorite UI state. 2) If the user opens the Favorite Gyms summary (triggering getFavoriteGyms) and then returns to the gym detail, the page can reconcile the current gym against the gyms returned by getFavoriteGyms to ensure the favorite indicator remains accurate without additional interfaces.
interactions: On page load or when the gymId URL parameter changes, the page triggers getGymDetails with the current gymId and updates all bound components when the response is received.

Clicking the favorite action button when the current gym is not already a favorite calls addGymToFavorites with the current gymId and updates the isFavorite state and any favorites-related UI elements upon a successful response.

Clicking the favorite action button when the gym is already marked as favorite either disables the call or provides non-destructive feedback without calling addGymToFavorites, preventing duplicate requests.
Clicking a control to view the Favorite Gyms list calls getFavoriteGyms, then displays or updates a favorites summary component that lists all gyms returned by the interface.

Clicking a gym entry in the Nearby Gyms list or in the favorites summary navigates the browser to gym_detail.html with that gym’s id set as the gymId URL parameter, which in turn triggers a fresh getGymDetails call for the selected gym.

Clicking Back to Results or Back to Gym Directory navigates the user to gyms.html, optionally including preserved search-related query parameters from the current URL to maintain context on the directory page.

If getGymDetails, getFavoriteGyms, or addGymToFavorites return an error or indicate failure, a status/message component displays an appropriate error state while leaving existing rendered data unchanged.
If getGymDetails returns no nearbyGyms or an empty array, the Nearby Gyms component hides its list or shows a controlled empty state message without any hardcoded placeholder data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from URL parameters, specifically gymId. When gym_detail.html loads, it reads gymId from the query string and calls getGymDetails(gymId) to populate the page. If gymId changes (for example, by clicking a nearby gym link that navigates to gym_detail.html?gymId=anotherId), the page re-runs getGymDetails for the new value and all components re-bind to the new Gym data. Favorite status is not stored in URL parameters; instead, isFavorite is read from the getGymDetails response and used to determine the visual state of the favorite action button and indicators. Optional navigation context or search parameters (such as those provided by gyms.html or index.html) can be carried forward in the query string when linking to gym_detail.html and back again to gyms.html, but the Gym Details page itself treats them as opaque values used only to construct back-navigation URLs (it does not call additional interfaces based on them). All persistent information about favorite gyms resides server-side and is accessed exclusively through getGymDetails, getFavoriteGyms, and addGymToFavorites; no localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. UI states such as loading, error, or temporary success messages are kept in in-memory page state and are reset when the page is reloaded or when gymId changes.

components.functionality: Reads the gymId parameter from the current page URL, calls getGymDetails(gymId) when the page loads or when gymId changes, and distributes the returned gym, isFavorite flag, and nearbyGyms array to other components via shared page state.

Displays key identifying information about the current gym such as its name and primary location fields, and optionally a compact indicator of whether it is currently in the user's favorites list, based on the Gym entity and isFavorite state provided by the data loader.

Shows the gym's contact-related fields from the Gym entity, including address lines, city, state, postal code, country, phone, email, and website URL, and may provide actionable links such as click-to-call, mailto, or external navigation to the website using the dynamic values from Gym.

Renders the list of programs associated with the gym by iterating over the Gym.programs array and indicates whether youth-oriented programs are available using the Gym.hasYouthPrograms flag, without hardcoding specific program names.

Provides a button that reflects the current favorite status of the displayed gym. When the gym is not yet a favorite, clicking the button calls addGymToFavorites with the current gymId. On a successful response, it updates the shared isFavorite state and may update any favorites-related components with the returned FavoriteGymsList while showing a confirmation message; on error, it shows an error message and leaves the state unchanged.

Optionally displays a summary view of the user's Favorite Gyms when requested. Upon user interaction to open the panel, it calls getFavoriteGyms, stores the returned favoriteGymsList and associated gyms array in local state, and renders a navigable list of Gym entries. Selecting a gym changes the page URL to gym_detail.html with the selected gym's id as gymId, which triggers the standard detail loading flow.

Displays the list of nearby gyms provided by the nearbyGyms field from the getGymDetails response, each represented as a Gym entity. For each item, it renders essential details (such as name and location) and a link that navigates to that gym's details by updating the gymId URL parameter. If the list is empty, it shows an appropriate empty state without static placeholder data.

Provides navigation controls such as Back to Results and Back to Gym Directory. It constructs the target URL to gyms.html and optionally appends any search-related query parameters present in the current URL so that the directory page can restore search context. It may also include a link to open the Favorite Gyms summary panel.

Centralizes display of transient status messages and errors for operations executed on this page, including data loading via getGymDetails, fetching favorites via getFavoriteGyms, and saving favorites via addGymToFavorites. It listens for success and error events from these actions and renders non-hardcoded, generic feedback messages accordingly.

Shows loading indicators while getGymDetails or favorites-related requests are in progress and displays an overall error state if the main gym details cannot be retrieved, guiding the user to try again or navigate back without relying on static sample content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. Header content (logo, nav, search) is aligned along this grid with clear left, center, and right zones.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom content flow under the existing global header (logo/nav/search). The gym detail page begins with a full-bleed gym header hero that mirrors the home hero: background photo, centered key text, and a bold pill-shaped primary CTA (favorite gym). Below this, content is organized in a main content column (left/center) and a supportive sidebar column (right) within a centered 12-column grid. Support sections (programs, contact, favorites summary, nearby gyms, navigation toolbar, status messages) are stacked in clearly separated horizontal bands, preserving the marketing-site feel rather than a dense app UI.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom content flow under the existing global header (logo/nav/search). The gym detail page begins with a full-bleed gym header hero that mirrors the home hero: background photo, centered key text, and a bold pill-shaped primary CTA (favorite gym). Below this, content is organized in a main content column (left/center) and a supportive sidebar column (right) within a centered 12-column grid. Support sections (programs, contact, favorites summary, nearby gyms, navigation toolbar, status messages) are stacked in clearly separated horizontal bands, preserving the marketing-site feel rather than a dense app UI.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The gym header hero occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport height, full-bleed horizontally with its main content constrained to the central 60–70% of page width. The remaining vertical space is divided into clearly separated sections: a prominent programs section taking most of the main column height, a shorter right-hand column for contact info and favorites summary, and a lower related-gyms band spanning the content width. Status messages and navigation toolbar occupy slim horizontal strips with 48–64px vertical separation from adjacent blocks, following the site’s spacious rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain the mockup’s moderate, marketing-style density: each section has generous padding (24–32px internal) and clear breathing room between modules. Text blocks are concise and legible with ample line height. No section feels crammed—lists (programs, nearby gyms) use plenty of vertical spacing between items (12–16px) and comfortable left/right padding within the content container. Panels (contact, favorites summary) are compact but not tight, with 24px internal padding and clear visual separation via card-style background or border.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow begins with the global header (logo → nav → search) at the very top, then drops immediately into the gym header hero: eye catches the gym name in large type near the center-left, then is drawn to the bold pill-shaped Favorite button slightly below and to the right, echoing the home CTA. From there, attention moves downward into the main gym content: programs list on the left/center, with occasional glances to the right-hand contact and favorites summary panel. Lower on the page, the nearby gyms list forms a visually distinct band that naturally follows after users finish scanning programs. Navigation toolbar and status messages are placed just above the main content area, so users see navigational and feedback cues right after grasping where they are (gym header).

layout.overall_description: Below the site-wide fixed header (logo on the left, horizontal navigation across the top center, search on the top right), the Gym Detail page opens with a wide, edge-to-edge gym hero section that fills roughly the top half of the viewport. A large background photograph of the gym or climbing scene stretches full width, slightly softened behind a central content band. Centered horizontally within the standard content container (around 70% of the page width), the gym’s name appears in large, bold type, with location and key metadata just below it. A prominent pill-shaped “Add to Favorites” button in the brand’s red sits just below and slightly to the right of the title block, echoing the primary CTA in the original mockup. Below this hero, the page shifts into a structured content area on a light background: a main content column on the left/center houses the detailed programs list and then, further down, a band of nearby gyms. To the right, a narrower sidebar column holds the gym’s contact panel and a concise favorites summary card. A slim navigation toolbar and any status messages sit at the top of this content region, beneath the hero, spanning most of the content width. Vertical spacing between sections is generous (roughly 48–64px), giving each block room to breathe, while everything aligns to an invisible 12-column grid, keeping the layout organized and professional. As users scroll, their attention naturally flows from the striking hero into actionable content (programs, contact, nearby options), supported by subtle but clear feedback and navigation elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gym-detail-loader appears in the main content area directly below the global header and, when active, temporarily replaces the gym-header-section and subsequent content. It spans the full width of the centered content container (approximately 70–80% of the page width, aligned to the 12-column grid) with generous top and bottom padding (24–32px) to maintain the spacious feel. On desktop, skeleton lines or loading placeholders for the hero title, CTA, and the main column blocks are centered horizontally within this container. Vertically, the loader occupies roughly the upper 50–60% of the viewport so that the layout still feels anchored and balanced, leaving some whitespace below rather than filling the entire screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the main content; when active, it pushes other components down or replaces them entirely in the DOM order. It does not fix or float; on slow connections, it appears almost immediately beneath the header and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gym-header-section functions as a hero band for the gym, starting immediately below the global header and spanning the full browser width edge-to-edge. It occupies roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with a large photographic background that is vertically centered. Within this hero, a centered content container (max-width around 1120–1200px, 70–80% of page width) is horizontally centered and padded internally by 24–32px. Inside this container, the gym name is positioned slightly left of true center—occupying about the central 40–50% of the width—with large, multi-line headline styling and generous line spacing. Directly beneath the name, smaller subtext (location, rating, membership type) runs on one to two lines, aligned to the headline’s left edge. The lower portion of this central content area reserves space for the favorite-gym-button, which is visually anchored below the headline block and slightly offset to the right, maintaining ample whitespace around it. The hero’s side areas (left and right 25–30% of the screen) are mostly just the photograph, providing visual breathing room similar to the original home hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level hero, not sticky. It scrolls away as the user moves down the page. On smaller screens, the height can compress to fit content, but it still appears as the first major section under the site header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gym-contact-section sits within the main content area below the hero, aligned to the right side of the content container as a sidebar panel. On desktop, as the content container breaks into a 12-column grid, the contact panel occupies roughly 3–4 columns on the right (about 25–30% of the content width), beginning approximately 48px below the bottom edge of the hero. It is visually grouped as a card with a subtle background or border, internal padding of 24px, and rounded corners, matching the clean, professional style. Vertically, it aligns near the top of the gym-programs-section in the main column, so that when users scan left to right they see programs on the left and contact info on the right. The panel’s content is stacked top-to-bottom: gym address at the top, followed by phone, email, website, and operating hours, each separated by 8–12px of vertical space to maintain clarity. On tablet and mobile, this section drops below the programs section, still centered within the container and spanning close to 100% of the content width with consistent padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls with the content. On very tall pages, it does not stick, but the consistent card shape and right alignment make it feel anchored in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gym-programs-section is the main content block immediately below the gym-header-section, occupying the primary left/center column. Within the centered content container, it starts around 48px below the hero’s bottom edge. On desktop, it spans roughly 7–8 of the 12 columns (about 60–65% of the content width), aligned to the left and leaving space on the right for the gym-contact-section and favorites-summary-panel. The section has a clear title at the top aligned to the left, with 16–24px of space below before the list of programs begins. The list itself is vertically stacked, with each program presented as a wide row or card spanning the full width of this column, separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing. Internal padding within each program item is around 16–24px, preserving the comfortable density of the mockup. The entire programs section has 24–32px of top and bottom padding internally to visually separate it from the background and neighboring sections. On smaller screens, the section expands to nearly full width and the right-hand sidebar content stacks below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static main-content block that scrolls normally. It defines the primary reading flow, so other components (contact, favorites summary) are positioned relative to its vertical start.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-gym-button is positioned inside the gym-header-section as the primary call-to-action. Within the hero’s central content band, it sits below the gym title and any short subtext, with about 24px of vertical separation. Horizontally, it is slightly right of the headline’s centerline—aligned so the left edge of the button lines up roughly under the headline’s midline, creating a subtle rightward pull similar to the home page’s main CTA. The button has a pill shape with generous padding (12–16px vertically and 24–32px horizontally) and stands out in the brand’s bold red, contrasting against the softened hero image. It occupies roughly the visual width of 20–25% of the content container on desktop, but feels centered within the hero’s focal area due to the symmetrical surrounding whitespace. On mobile, the button centers directly under the gym name, spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the hero’s text stack; it does not float or stick. Its position is visually anchored toward the lower-center of the hero content so that as users’ eyes move down from the headline, the button is the next obvious focal point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-summary-panel appears in the same right-hand column as the gym-contact-section, positioned directly beneath it with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans the same width as the contact card (roughly 3–4 grid columns or 25–30% of the content width on desktop), creating a visually aligned sidebar stack. The panel presents a concise summary of the user’s favorite gyms—such as count and a short list of key favorites—inside a card with 24px internal padding, following the same card style as the contact section for visual cohesion. It is slightly shorter in height than the contact card, making it feel like a compact, supportive widget. On mobile, it stacks below the contact panel and spans nearly full width, with equal outer margins as other sections and consistent vertical spacing (24–32px) separating it from surrounding content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar card; it scrolls with the content and remains visually grouped with the contact section. It does not stick to the viewport but maintains a consistent relationship beneath the contact panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nearby-gyms-list forms its own horizontal band below the main content sections (programs, contact, favorites summary). It begins approximately 64px beneath the bottom of the gym-programs-section, spanning the full width of the content container and centered on the page. On desktop, it uses the full 12-column content width, with a section heading aligned to the left and the list of nearby gyms arranged either as a single-column vertical list or a responsive grid of cards (e.g., two to three cards per row), depending on available screen width. Each gym item has consistent internal padding (16–24px) and 16–24px spacing between items, keeping the density moderate. This section visually reads as a “related content” band, with perhaps a subtly different background tone to differentiate it from the main details above, but still fitting within the marketing-style rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that sits below the primary detail content. It scrolls with the page and does not overlap or float; it serves as a natural continuation of the user’s downward exploration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-toolbar is a slim horizontal band of text-based links (e.g., Back to Directory, Back to Results, Next Gym) positioned just below the gym-header-section and above the main content blocks. It sits within the same centered content container and spans nearly its full width, with 16–24px of vertical padding and around 48px of whitespace separating it from both the hero above and the programs/contact content below. The links themselves are arranged in a single line aligned to the left or center, with 24–32px of horizontal spacing between them, maintaining the clean, airy navigation feel from the mockup’s header nav. On smaller screens, links can wrap to a second line while keeping generous line height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline section. Optionally, on larger screens, it can become sticky within the content area (sticking to the top once scrolled into view) if additional wayfinding is needed, but by default it scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-status-messages area resides directly below the navigation-toolbar and above the main gym content sections. It occupies the full width of the content container and appears as a slim but clearly visible band or stacked alerts. Each message has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with rounded corners and a background color indicating message type (success, info, error) while staying consistent with the site’s professional tone. Vertically, there is a small 16–24px gap between the navigation-toolbar and the first message, and a 32–48px gap between the last message and the gym-programs-section. On pages without active messages, this area collapses, preserving the clean layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, appearing only when there are messages to show. Messages scroll away with the rest of the content and do not overlay or fix to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error-state component appears in the central content area at the same vertical level where the gym-programs-section would normally begin, centered horizontally within the content container. For loading states not covered by the gym-detail-loader, it shows a centered indicator (spinner or icon) with short explanatory text, using 16–24px top and bottom padding around the block. In an error state (e.g., gym not found), it expands slightly to occupy a visually substantial card centered in the container, with 24–32px internal padding, a clear error headline, and supporting text plus an action (such as a link back to the directory). This card spans roughly 50–60% of the content width on desktop and is surrounded by generous whitespace (48–64px) above and below, mirroring the hero’s clear focal treatment but at a smaller scale.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block that conditionally replaces or overlays the main content sections in the reading order. It does not fix to the viewport but appears prominently in the main content column, pushing other sections down or being shown instead of them when appropriate.

name: Job Listings
title: Job Listings
description: Page to search, filter, sort, and browse job postings for climbing professionals, with filter state controlled by URL parameters and data loaded through job filter and search interfaces.

core_features: Load available job filter and sort options from the getJobFilterOptions interface and initialize filter controls (role, region, minimum salary, salary unit, sort).
Parse URL parameters (role, region, min_salary, salary_unit, sort, and pagination parameters) to initialize the current filter and sort state when the page loads or when the URL changes.
Search and filter job postings via the searchJobPostings interface using the current filter, sort, and pagination state, and render results as a list of job cards.
Allow users to change filters (role, region, minimum salary, salary unit) and sorting, updating URL parameters accordingly and re-triggering searchJobPostings.
Provide pagination controls based on the totalCount returned by searchJobPostings, updating pagination-related URL parameters and re-running the search.
Display a summary of the current results (e.g., total results and active filters) based on searchJobPostings.totalCount and the filter state.
Handle loading and error states for both getJobFilterOptions and searchJobPostings to provide feedback while data is being fetched or when requests fail.
Allow navigation to job_detail.html when a user clicks a job card, passing the corresponding jobPostingId from the selected JobPosting entity through URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Basic browsing from navigation
1) User selects the 'Careers' or 'Job Board' navigation link.
2) Browser opens jobs.html without explicit query parameters.
3) Page controller parses the URL (no filters present) and initializes empty filter state.
4) Page calls getJobFilterOptions to load roles, regions, salaryUnits, and sortOptions.
5) Once options are loaded, filter controls are populated and default values are selected (e.g., first sort option when no sort parameter is provided).
6) Page calls searchJobPostings with the current state (no filters, default sort, initial pagination).
7) Job cards are rendered from searchJobPostings.jobPostings.
8) User scrolls and optionally uses pagination controls to move between result pages; each pagination action updates URL pagination parameters and re-calls searchJobPostings.

Workflow 2: Arriving with pre-configured filters from index.html
1) User interacts with a job teaser or quick filter on index.html and is sent to jobs.html with query parameters such as role, region, min_salary, salary_unit, and sort.
2) Page controller reads these URL parameters and initializes the filter and sort state accordingly.
3) Page calls getJobFilterOptions and, once loaded, attempts to match URL values to the available option values (e.g., role parameter is matched against getJobFilterOptions.roles[*].value). Invalid or unmatched parameter values are ignored in the UI while kept as-is in the URL or normalized according to implementation rules.
4) Filter controls display the selected options that correspond to the URL parameters (e.g., the role dropdown shows the option whose value matches the role parameter).
5) Page calls searchJobPostings using the normalized filter and sort state derived from the URL.
6) Results are displayed already filtered and sorted, aligned with the user’s intent from index.html.
7) If the user adjusts any filter or sort control, the URL parameters are updated and searchJobPostings is called again with the new state.

Workflow 3: Updating filters and sorting within the Job Listings page
1) User changes one or more filters in the job filter form (e.g., selects a different role option, region option, adjusts minimum salary, or changes salary unit).
2) On filter form submission (or on change, depending on implementation), the page controller updates the URL query parameters (role, region, min_salary, salary_unit) to reflect the new filter values, resets pagination to the first page, and leaves other unrelated parameters intact.
3) Page controller calls searchJobPostings with the updated state.
4) Job listing results refresh to show postings that match the new filters.
5) User changes the sort option using the sort control; the sort parameter in the URL is updated and searchJobPostings is called again with the new sortBy value while preserving current filters.
6) Back/forward browser navigation changes the URL; a URL-change listener re-parses parameters and re-runs searchJobPostings so the UI stays consistent with browser history.

Workflow 4: Navigating to job details from a listing
1) After reviewing the job cards, the user clicks on a specific job card or its dedicated link.
2) The click event handler reads the selected JobPosting.id from the jobPostings array.
3) The browser is navigated to job_detail.html with a jobPostingId query parameter set to the selected JobPosting.id (e.g., job_detail.html?jobPostingId={id}).
4) The Job Listings page does not handle job details; the job_detail.html page is responsible for loading and presenting full job information.

Workflow 5: Sharing and revisiting filtered job searches
1) User adjusts filters and sorting until the job list matches their interests; each change updates the URL parameters (role, region, min_salary, salary_unit, sort, plus pagination parameters when applicable).
2) User copies the URL from the address bar or bookmarks the page.
3) Later, the same user or another user opens this URL.
4) Page controller reads all provided query parameters and initializes filters and pagination exactly as encoded in the URL.
5) getJobFilterOptions is invoked, filter controls are set to reflect the stored parameters, and searchJobPostings is called with the same filters, sort, and pagination so that the same type of result set is shown (subject to underlying data changes over time).
interactions: Changing the role filter: User selects an option from the role dropdown populated from getJobFilterOptions.roles. Event handler updates the internal role filter state, synchronizes the role parameter in the URL to the selected option’s value, resets pagination to the first page, and triggers a new searchJobPostings call.

Changing the region filter: User selects an option from the region dropdown based on getJobFilterOptions.regions. Event handler updates the region filter state, updates the region URL parameter, resets pagination, and triggers searchJobPostings.

Entering a minimum salary value: User types a numeric value into the minimum salary input field. On form submission, the event handler validates the input as a number, writes it into the min_salary URL parameter (or removes that parameter if cleared), resets pagination, and calls searchJobPostings with minSalary set to the parsed number.

Changing the salary unit: User picks a salary unit option from the dropdown using getJobFilterOptions.salaryUnits. Event handler sets the salaryUnit state, updates salary_unit in the URL to the selected value, and re-runs searchJobPostings with salaryUnit set accordingly.

Changing sort order: User selects a sort option from the sort control initialized with getJobFilterOptions.sortOptions. Event handler updates the sort state, writes the corresponding value into the sort URL parameter, keeps the current filters and page (or optionally resets pagination), and triggers searchJobPostings with sortBy set to this value.

Submitting the filter form: User clicks an 'Apply filters' (or equivalent) button on the filter form. Event handler aggregates all filter control values into a normalized state, updates the URL parameters (role, region, min_salary, salary_unit, sort) in a single navigation action, resets pagination, and calls searchJobPostings with a single consolidated request.

Using pagination controls: User clicks 'Next', 'Previous', or a specific page number in pagination controls. Event handler calculates the new pagination parameters (such as page index and derived offset), updates these in the URL, and calls searchJobPostings with corresponding offset and limit while preserving current filter and sort parameters.

Handling page load or URL changes: When the page loads or the browser’s address bar changes (including back/forward navigation), a URL listener parses query parameters, updates the filter and sort state, then calls getJobFilterOptions if needed and searchJobPostings with the reconstructed state so that UI components reflect the URL.

Clicking a job card: User clicks on a job card or an embedded link. Event handler reads the JobPosting.id for that card, constructs a URL to job_detail.html with jobPostingId set to that id, and navigates the browser to that URL.

Handling loading state: When either getJobFilterOptions or searchJobPostings is in progress, the page sets an internal loading flag. The loading indicator component uses this flag to render a loading state (e.g., disabling controls or showing a non-static loading message) until the API response is received.

Handling error state: If getJobFilterOptions or searchJobPostings returns an error or cannot be reached, the page sets an error state. An error component reads this error state and shows an error message derived from the response or a generic message; controls remain available so the user can retry by resubmitting filters or refreshing the page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Job Listings page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter, sort, and pagination state; there is no use of localStorage or other persistent client storage. On every page load and on any URL change, the page controller parses the following parameters:
- role (string): When present and matching one of getJobFilterOptions.roles[*].value, it sets the selected role filter. If it does not match any available value, the UI may show no selection for role while role is still present in the URL until normalized or cleared.
- region (string): When present and matching getJobFilterOptions.regions[*].value, it sets the selected region filter. Non-matching values are handled similarly to role.
- min_salary (number): When present and successfully parsed as a number, it sets the minimum salary filter (minSalary). If missing or invalid, no minimum salary filter is applied.
- salary_unit (string): When present and matching getJobFilterOptions.salaryUnits[*].value, it sets the salaryUnit filter. If not present or invalid, the page omits this filter or uses a configured default that does not conflict with the interface’s available values.
- sort (string): When present and matching getJobFilterOptions.sortOptions[*].value, it sets the current sortBy value. If absent or invalid, a default sort is chosen (for example, the first available sortOptions entry) without hardcoding a specific domain value.
- Pagination parameters (e.g., page and any additional numeric parameters such as offset) are interpreted by the controller. A configured page size (limit) is used to derive the offset parameter passed into searchJobPostings; offset is computed as page index multiplied by limit. If pagination parameters are missing, the controller uses initial defaults (such as the first page and a configured page size).
The controller composes a search request object for searchJobPostings with role, region, minSalary, salaryUnit, sortBy, limit, and offset strictly derived from the current URL-driven state. Whenever the user changes filters, sorting, or pagination through UI components, the controller updates the URL query parameters using browser navigation APIs (e.g., pushing a new history state) and then re-computes and issues a new searchJobPostings call based on the new URL. This ensures that the visible listing always reflects the URL parameters and that browser navigation and shared links reproduce the same functional state of the page.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing and synchronization, and all calls to getJobFilterOptions and searchJobPostings. Maintains in-memory state for filters, sorting, pagination, loading, and errors, and exposes this state to other components.
data_binding: URLParameters(role, region, min_salary, salary_unit, sort, pagination)

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls for filtering job postings by role, region, minimum salary, and salary unit. Initializes control values from the current URL-derived state and updates URL parameters and search state when the user applies or changes filters.
data_binding: URLParameters(role, region, min_salary, salary_unit) mapped to initial control values

components.functionality: Allows users to choose the sort order for job results using the sort options returned by getJobFilterOptions. Keeps the selected sort value synchronized with the sort URL parameter and triggers refreshed searches when changed.
data_binding: URLParameters(sort) mapped to the initially selected sort option

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of the current job search results, such as total results count and a textual representation of active filters, based on searchJobPostings.totalCount and the controller’s filter state.

Renders the array of job postings returned from searchJobPostings as a list of clickable job cards. Each card shows key attributes from the JobPosting entity (such as title, location fields, salary fields, and postedDate) and provides the interaction to navigate to the job detail page.

Represents a single JobPosting in the list. Extracts and displays data such as JobPosting.title, job location-related fields (e.g., JobPosting.city, JobPosting.state, JobPosting.country), salaryMin, salaryMax, salaryCurrency, salaryUnit, postedDate, and isRemote, without hardcoded labels or values. Reacts to click events to navigate to job_detail.html with the jobPostingId parameter.

Provides controls to navigate through multiple pages of job results. Uses totalCount from searchJobPostings and the current limit and offset maintained by the controller to compute available pages and which controls are active. Updates URL pagination parameters and triggers new searches when the user changes pages.
data_binding: URLParameters(pagination such as page and derived offset)

components.functionality: Shows or hides a loading state while the page is waiting for responses from getJobFilterOptions or searchJobPostings. Can also disable certain interactions (e.g., filter form submit) while requests are in-flight, based on controller loading flags.

Displays error information when calls to getJobFilterOptions or searchJobPostings fail. Reads structured error details from the controller state and presents them in a consistent way, allowing the user to retry actions via existing controls.

Listens for changes in browser URL (including back/forward navigation) and updates the controller’s state to match the current query parameters. Also, when filters, sorting, or pagination change via UI components, this component is responsible for encoding those changes into URL query parameters without introducing any additional persistence layer.
data_binding: URLParameters(role, region, min_salary, salary_unit, sort, pagination)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. Header content (logo, nav, search) is aligned along this grid with clear left, center, and right zones.
layout.layout_pattern: Continue the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. The global header and hero remain consistent with the rest of the site. The Job Listings content begins immediately below the hero as a centered main column on a 12-column grid. Within this main area, the sequence is: a subtle page-heading band (handled by the controller contextually), then a prominent, full-width filter/search form band, followed by a compact control strip (results summary left, sort control right), then the vertically stacked list of job cards, and finally pagination at the bottom. Error and loading states occupy the same vertical slot as the list, preserving overall flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Continue the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. The global header and hero remain consistent with the rest of the site. The Job Listings content begins immediately below the hero as a centered main column on a 12-column grid. Within this main area, the sequence is: a subtle page-heading band (handled by the controller contextually), then a prominent, full-width filter/search form band, followed by a compact control strip (results summary left, sort control right), then the vertically stacked list of job cards, and finally pagination at the bottom. Error and loading states occupy the same vertical slot as the list, preserving overall flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero at the top of the page maintains the ~80% of initial viewport height feel on first load. Below it, the Job Listings section uses a centered content container spanning roughly 8–10 of 12 columns (about 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop). Within this section, the filter form is the main functional focal area and occupies the first 20–25% of that content height with a visually prominent card-like band. The results summary + sort strip occupies a shallow band directly under the filters. The job list consumes the majority of the remaining space, with job cards stacked vertically and breathing room of 24–32px between cards. Pagination occupies a shallow band at the bottom with generous top margin (about 48px) to clearly separate it from the last card.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: each major block (filter form, control strip, list, pagination) is clearly separated by 32–48px vertical spacing. The filter form uses comfortable internal padding (24–32px) and arranges controls in 1–2 horizontal rows, but avoids an overly busy feel by prioritizing a main keyword search and a small set of primary filters. Job cards themselves use 24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between text lines, keeping them readable and scannable. Microcopy (results summary, error messages) is compact and aligned to the grid edges to avoid clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a Z/F pattern borrowed from the mockup. After the site header and hero, attention drops to the left-aligned section title and primary filter/search area. The main keyword input sits prominently in the upper portion of the filter band, spanning most of the width, acting as the first focal point. The eye then moves right to any secondary filter controls and the submit/clear actions. From there, the gaze naturally travels down to the results summary on the left and the sort control on the right, forming a shallow Z-line. Finally, the eye flows down through the vertically stacked job cards to the centered pagination controls. Error or loading states briefly dominate the central list area when present, but do not disrupt the overall left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading pattern.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header and full-bleed hero, the Job Listings page content sits in a centered column aligned to a 12‑column grid, roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop with generous side margins. The first thing visible in this section is a clean, card-like filter band stretching across the content width: a large, pill-edged keyword search field anchors the upper left-to-center, with supporting filters and a bold, brand-colored ‘Search Jobs’ or ‘Apply Filters’ action pill toward the right, all cushioned by 24–32px of internal padding and at least 32px of whitespace above and below. Immediately beneath this, a slim control strip runs across the same content width: on the left, a small line of text summarizing the number of results; on the right, a compact sort dropdown aligned to the same right edge as the filter action, creating a clear, horizontal balance. Below that control strip, a vertical column of job cards fills the page, each full-width within the container, separated by 24–32px, with subtle card backgrounds, 24px internal padding, and strong, left-aligned job titles that guide scanning down the page. At the very bottom, centered pagination pills echo the visual language of the CTA buttons, with ample breathing room above them so they feel like a distinct, navigational band rather than part of the last card. Loading indicators and error messages appear inline where the list would be, occupying the central area so the user’s focus remains on the main content zone. Overall, the layout feels modern and professional, borrowing the hero’s sense of spaciousness and strong brand accents while providing a clear, linear workflow from filtering to browsing to paginating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-page-controller has no visual footprint. It sits logically at the top of the Job Listings section in the DOM, just below the hero region, orchestrating data loading, URL parameter parsing, and filter state. From a layout perspective, it simply ensures that when the main content area (filters, controls, list) renders, it is aligned to the same centered content container as the rest of the site. It does not occupy any visible width or height and does not alter spacing; instead, it governs when the loading indicator, error display, filters, and list are shown within the established vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller component with no direct spatial presence; affects which visible components render but does not affect layout spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-filter-form is the first visible functional component of the Job Listings section and is treated as a prominent, full-width band. It sits immediately below any page title text for the section and is separated from the hero or title by about 48px of whitespace. The form is contained within the centered main content area, spanning roughly 8–10 of 12 columns (70–80% width), and visually appears as a light card with rounded corners and a subtle shadow or border. Internally it has 24–32px padding on all sides. On desktop, the top row contains a large, pill-shaped keyword input that starts near the left grid edge and spans about 60–70% of the form’s width, with its height clearly taller than typical inputs (around 40–48px) to echo the bold pill buttons used in the hero. To the right of this input, aligned to the same baseline, sit key action buttons like ‘Search’ and ‘Clear’ in a similar pill style, using the brand red for the primary action and a neutral outline for secondary actions. Below, or immediately beneath the keyword bar, a second row assembles secondary filters (e.g., location, job type, employment type) into 2–3 columns where space allows, each occupying about 1/3 of the width, with 16–24px horizontal gaps and 16–24px vertical spacing from the row above. The entire block is separated from the control strip below by at least 32px of whitespace, reinforcing its role as a distinct control panel. On smaller screens, the filter elements stack vertically, maintaining 16–24px spacing between fields while keeping the pill-shaped action button pinned near the bottom of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow block-level section within the main content; non-sticky by default but visually grouped as a single card band. On narrow screens, filters wrap and stack while maintaining the same outer margins and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-sort-control appears as a compact dropdown or segmented control aligned to the right side of the content column, directly beneath the job-filter-form. It shares a horizontal row with the job-results-summary: the summary text is left-aligned, and the sort control is right-aligned, both sitting on the same baseline. This row sits about 24px below the bottom edge of the filter form card, creating a clear yet tight connection between filtering and sorting. The sort control itself occupies roughly 15–20% of the content width, with a minimum width to keep its label fully visible, and its height visually matches other interactive elements (around 32–40px). The control uses subtle styling—light border or pill-shape—with a small down arrow, drawing attention through alignment and proximity rather than large size. On smaller screens, the sort control drops below the results summary, still right-aligned within its own line but maintaining 16–24px vertical spacing between the two lines.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the results-summary within a horizontal control strip; participates in responsive wrapping so that on narrow screens it stacks below but remains right-aligned within the container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-results-summary is a concise line of text located directly under the job-filter-form and aligned flush with the left edge of the content grid. It sits on the same horizontal row as the job-sort-control, occupying the left portion (about 50–60% of the width) of that row. The text is small to medium in size and low visual weight compared to headings—think a subdued gray rather than pure black—so it reads as supportive metadata. A vertical spacing of roughly 24px separates it from the bottom edge of the filter form, and another 24px separates this control strip from the top of the job-list below. This keeps the summary legible and connected to the filters and list without crowding. On narrow screens, the summary remains at the top of the control area, left-aligned and taking the full width above the sort control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element in a horizontal control strip, flowing naturally with text; on small screens, it becomes the first line of a two-line control block above the sort control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-list occupies the main central area of the Job Listings section, directly beneath the results summary and sort control strip. It spans the full width of the content container (about 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop), with its left edge aligned to the same vertical grid line as the summary and filters. Within this region, job cards are stacked vertically in a single column, each card occupying nearly the entire width of the list area with 24–32px of vertical spacing between them. The top of the first card starts about 24px below the control strip, emphasizing a clear yet tight connection between the controls and the results. As the user scrolls, this stacked column becomes the primary scrolling content, stretching down the page while maintaining consistent left and right margins. On larger screens, the list does not split into multiple columns to preserve readability; on smaller screens, the cards simply adapt their internal layout but the overall one-column stacking pattern remains intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertical flow region that expands as more results are loaded; scrollable as part of the main page content with no fixed or sticky behavior applied directly to the list itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each job-card is a clickable card-style element within the job-list, designed to be highly scannable. It fills almost the entire width of the content column (leaving only the standard inner padding from the container edges) and is separated from neighboring cards by 24–32px of vertical whitespace. The card’s background is slightly elevated from the page—either via a subtle shadow or a light, contrasting background—paired with rounded corners that echo the pill shapes of buttons. Internal padding is approximately 24px on all sides. The layout inside the card follows a left-to-right, top-to-bottom hierarchy: at the top left, the job title appears in larger, bold text occupying roughly 70% of the card width, while on the top right there is room for a small badge (e.g., ‘New’, ‘Featured’) or a compact pill tag. Below the title, aligned to the left, a second row displays the company name and location, arranged in a single line or two stacked lines depending on content length. Under that, a third row holds metadata (e.g., job type, posted date) in smaller, muted text or pill-style tags, usually running horizontally and wrapping as needed. The bottom part of the card can include a brief one- or two-line description, with about 8–16px spacing above it, ensuring the card remains relatively shallow and easy to scan. The entire card behaves as a hoverable/tappable surface, with hover states (e.g., slight elevation or border color change) that align with the site’s modern, minimal style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level card within the list, expanding vertically based on content but constrained to the list’s column width; no fixed behavior, simply participates in the standard stacked flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-pagination-controls sit at the very bottom of the Job Listings section, underneath the last job card. They are separated from the final card by a generous 48px of vertical whitespace, creating a distinct navigation band that signals the end of the current page of results. The pagination controls are centered horizontally within the content container, forming a compact horizontal row of pill-shaped page buttons. The current page is indicated by a solid brand-colored pill, while other pages use outlined or neutral pills, echoing the styling of CTAs in a smaller form. ‘Previous’ and ‘Next’ controls, if present, flank the page numbers or sit slightly offset to the left and right, but remain within the same visual row. On smaller screens, the pagination row remains centered, and excess page numbers may collapse into fewer visible buttons with ellipses, keeping the control compact. A modest amount of whitespace (32–48px) below the pagination separates it from any footer content or subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Centered, block-level navigation row at the bottom of the results area; participates in standard flow and does not stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-loading-indicator appears within the same central column where the job-list would normally be, ensuring continuity of focus. When the page first loads or when filters/sort change, the list area is temporarily replaced or overlaid by the loading indicator. On desktop, the indicator is centered both horizontally and within the visible portion of the list area: typically midway between the control strip above and the pagination area below, or centered in the viewport if the list is empty yet. This could be a circular spinner or a linear progress bar accompanied by a short status message, both kept compact and using the brand color as an accent. Surrounding whitespace is preserved (24–32px above and below relative to controls and future list space) so the indicator feels integrated rather than jammed. On smaller screens, it remains centered horizontally with full-width room for any text label.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily occupies or overlays the job-list area; appears and disappears based on loading state without permanently affecting the layout structure. No fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-error-display appears as a prominent but slim alert band within the main content column, positioned directly above the job-list area and below the filter form and control strip. When an error occurs (e.g., network or server issue), the error band pushes the list downward, occupying its own vertical space with about 16–24px margin above and below. It spans the full width of the content container, aligned to the same grid edges as the filters and cards. The styling uses a subtle but clear error treatment: a light tinted background with a stronger left border or small icon in the brand’s alert color (likely a red variant) on the left, followed by concise error text and a possible retry action link or button on the right. The height of this band is relatively shallow—around 48–64px depending on content—preserving the page’s spacious feel while ensuring the error is immediately visible within the established reading flow. On narrow screens, the text and action stack vertically within the band but maintain internal padding of 16–24px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline alert that appears in the normal vertical flow, pushing the job-list down while visible; dismissed or hidden without leaving extra blank space when not present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-url-sync component has no visual representation on the page. It resides logically alongside the job-page-controller, listening for changes in the filter form, sort control, pagination, and other relevant state, and synchronizing that state with the URL parameters. From a layout perspective, it is invisible and does not claim any space in the grid. Its only impact is behavioral: when the URL changes, it triggers updates to the visible components within the existing spatial structure, without shifting or resizing any visible elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual utility component with no spatial footprint; operates entirely behind the scenes without affecting visible layout or spacing.

name: Job Detail & Application
title: Job Detail & Application
description: Displays full details for a single job posting based on a URL parameter and provides an application flow using a saved professional profile, including submission and confirmation.

core_features: Read the jobPostingId from the URL and load the corresponding job posting using getJobDetails(jobPostingId).

Display complete job information from the JobPosting model, including title, organization, location, region, salary range, posted date, remote flag, description, requirements, and application deadline.
Provide a clearly labeled 'Apply with Saved Profile' action that is distinct from any external application options and opens a dedicated application panel or modal.
On demand, load the user's available saved profiles using getSavedProfiles() for use in the application flow.
Allow the user to select one SavedProfile, optionally enter a message to the employer, and submit an application using applyToJobWithSavedProfile(jobPostingId, savedProfileId, messageToEmployer).
Show success or failure feedback after submission, including status and any returned message, using data from the JobApplication object returned by applyToJobWithSavedProfile.
Provide navigation back to the job listings page (jobs.html) via a link or breadcrumb that does not modify application data.
user_workflows:
View job details from listings:
1. User clicks a job in jobs.html (or index.html) which links to job_detail.html with a jobPostingId URL parameter.
2. Job Detail & Application page initializes and reads jobPostingId from the URL.
3. The page calls getJobDetails(jobPostingId).
4. While loading, the page shows a loading state instead of job content.
5. When getJobDetails returns successfully, the page populates all job details sections with the JobPosting data.
6. If getJobDetails fails (e.g., invalid jobPostingId), the page shows an error state and hides application actions.

Apply with a saved profile:
1. With job details visible, the user clicks the 'Apply with Saved Profile' button.
2. If saved profiles are not yet loaded, the page calls getSavedProfiles().
3. While getSavedProfiles is in progress, the application panel or modal shows a loading state instead of the profile list.
4. When savedProfiles are returned:
   - If one or more SavedProfile records exist, the component displays them as selectable options (e.g., radio buttons or list items).
   - If no SavedProfile records exist, the component displays a message explaining that no saved profiles are available and disables the submission action.
5. The user selects one saved profile from the list.
6. The user can optionally enter text into a 'Message to employer' field.
7. The user submits the application form.
8. The page validates that a savedProfileId is selected and that jobPostingId is available from the URL.
9. The page calls applyToJobWithSavedProfile(jobPostingId, savedProfileId, messageToEmployer) and shows a submitting state.
10. If the API call succeeds with success=true, the page:
    - Stores the returned JobApplication in page state.
    - Shows a success banner or inline confirmation using JobApplication.status and/or message.
    - Optionally closes the modal or switches the application panel to a read-only 'Application submitted' view.
11. If the API call returns success=false or fails, the page shows an error message and keeps the form open so the user can retry.

Handle expired or unavailable applications:
1. When job details are loaded, the page compares the current time with JobPosting.applicationDeadline (if provided).
2. If the applicationDeadline exists and is in the past, the 'Apply with Saved Profile' button is disabled or hidden.
3. The page shows text indicating that applications are closed.
4. If getJobDetails returns a JobPosting that cannot be applied to (based on business rules such as missing jobPostingId or other constraints), the page hides the application components.

Return to job listings:
1. At any time, the user can click the 'Back to Job Listings' link or a breadcrumb pointing to jobs.html.
2. The browser navigates to jobs.html without calling any additional interfaces from this page.
3. No application state is stored beyond the lifetime of the current page view; navigation away does not persist state through localStorage.
interactions: On page load, read the jobPostingId from the URL query string and trigger getJobDetails(jobPostingId).
If jobPostingId is missing from the URL, show a user-friendly error and do not attempt to call getJobDetails.
Click 'Apply with Saved Profile' button to open the application panel or modal and (if needed) trigger getSavedProfiles().
Within the application panel/modal, select a saved profile from the list of SavedProfile items (single selection required).
Type an optional message to the employer in a free-text field bound to the messageToEmployer parameter.
Click 'Submit Application' to validate the selection and message, then call applyToJobWithSavedProfile(jobPostingId, savedProfileId, messageToEmployer).
On successful application submission, display a success banner or message using the JobApplication response and update the application component to a submitted state.
On application submission failure, display an error message and keep the application form available so the user can adjust and resubmit.
Click 'Back to Job Listings' or a breadcrumb link to navigate back to jobs.html using standard URL navigation.

If an external application URL is present in JobPosting.applicationUrl, clicking an 'Apply Externally' link opens that URL in accordance with normal link behavior, without using any additional interfaces.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page-level state is driven by URL parameters, specifically jobPostingId. On initial load, the page extracts jobPostingId from the query string of job_detail.html (e.g., job_detail.html?jobPostingId=...). If jobPostingId is present, the page calls getJobDetails(jobPostingId) and stores the returned JobPosting in memory for binding to components. If jobPostingId is absent or empty, the page does not call getJobDetails and instead shows an error state indicating that the job cannot be loaded. If the user navigates to a different job by changing the URL (for example, via in-app navigation that updates the query string), the page detects the change in jobPostingId, re-runs getJobDetails with the new value, and refreshes all job detail components. Saved profiles and job applications are not stored in URL parameters; they are loaded and managed in volatile page state only. Application submission status is derived from the response to applyToJobWithSavedProfile and is used to conditionally render success or error messages; it is not persisted in the URL or localStorage. Navigating away from the page (for example, to jobs.html) clears this volatile state and reloading the same URL re-triggers getJobDetails using the jobPostingId parameter.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading jobPostingId from the URL and loading the corresponding job details. Manages loading, success, and error states for job data.
Displays key job information at the top of the page, such as job title, organization name, location, region, posted date, and remote indicator, using data from the loaded JobPosting.

Shows structured job metadata including salary range, salary unit, currency, application deadline, and region. Optionally displays an external application link when JobPosting.applicationUrl is present. Also determines whether the internal 'Apply with Saved Profile' button should be enabled based on the application deadline and any job-specific eligibility rules.
Displays the main job description and requirements using JobPosting.description and JobPosting.requirements, providing scrollable or sectioned content for users to review before applying.
Provides a breadcrumb or simple navigation link back to the job listings page (jobs.html). Does not make API calls and relies solely on standard navigation behavior.
Renders the primary 'Apply with Saved Profile' call-to-action button. When clicked, checks whether the job is open for applications and, if so, opens the saved profile application panel or modal.
On demand, loads the user's saved profiles using getSavedProfiles() when the application panel or modal is first opened. Manages loading, success, and empty-state handling for SavedProfile data.

Within the application panel or modal, lists the available saved profiles returned by getSavedProfiles(), allows the user to select one SavedProfile as the profile to apply with, and binds the selection to be used when calling applyToJobWithSavedProfile.
Provides a multi-line input for the user to enter an optional message to the employer. The value is bound to the messageToEmployer parameter sent to applyToJobWithSavedProfile.

Coordinates the submission of a job application using the selected saved profile and optional message. Validates that a SavedProfile has been selected and that jobPostingId is available, then calls applyToJobWithSavedProfile(jobPostingId, savedProfileId, messageToEmployer). Manages submitting state and routes the response to the application status components.

Displays feedback about the application submission, using the JobApplication object and the message and success indicators returned by applyToJobWithSavedProfile. Shows a success state when the application is submitted and a failure state with retry guidance when submission fails.

Centralizes logic to determine whether application actions should be enabled, considering factors such as the availability of JobPosting data, application deadline, presence of at least one SavedProfile, and current submission status. Exposes flags used by the apply button and application form to enable or disable interactions.

Handles and displays error states for the page, including missing jobPostingId in the URL, failure to load job details from getJobDetails, or other unrecoverable conditions. When active, it hides or disables application-related components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. Header content (logo, nav, search) is aligned along this grid with clear left, center, and right zones.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global fixed header, the page follows a linear, top-to-bottom marketing layout. At the top of the content area, a slim breadcrumb row anchors the user, followed immediately by a full-width hero band for the job detail header. This hero mirrors the membership landing hero: a wide visual area (solid color or softened photo) with centrally weighted content, large title text, key job metadata, and a bold pill-shaped 'Apply with saved profile' CTA. Below the hero, the page flows into two stacked blocks: first the job description, then the application section with the saved profile form, both aligned to the same centered content container and 12-column grid. Status/error banners occupy reserved horizontal bands near the top of the content area so they are seen before the main reading content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global fixed header, the page follows a linear, top-to-bottom marketing layout. At the top of the content area, a slim breadcrumb row anchors the user, followed immediately by a full-width hero band for the job detail header. This hero mirrors the membership landing hero: a wide visual area (solid color or softened photo) with centrally weighted content, large title text, key job metadata, and a bold pill-shaped 'Apply with saved profile' CTA. Below the hero, the page flows into two stacked blocks: first the job description, then the application section with the saved profile form, both aligned to the same centered content container and 12-column grid. Status/error banners occupy reserved horizontal bands near the top of the content area so they are seen before the main reading content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header maintains its shallow, efficient band at the very top. Within the viewport, the job hero section for the detail header occupies roughly the top 45–55% of the visible area on desktop, creating an impactful introduction without obscuring that more content exists below. The breadcrumb and status banners use minimal vertical space (about one grid row each) but span the container width, clearly separated by 16–24px of whitespace. Below the hero, the job description section takes most of the central vertical space, with generous top and bottom margins (~48px). The application form occupies a similarly tall block beneath the description, with enough padding (24–32px) around its card-like container to feel substantial but not overwhelming. Side margins keep content to about 70–80% of the viewport width on large screens, centered on a 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps content light and readable. The hero uses large typography with generous line height and ample whitespace between the title, metadata, and the main CTA. Metadata is concise and arranged in one or two horizontal rows to avoid clutter. The job description flows as a single column with comfortable line length and clear section breaks (heading, subheadings, lists) separated by 16–24px. The application form is broken into clear, stacked units (selection, message, submit) with 16–24px spacing between them so the process feels straightforward and approachable. No area is crammed; every interactive element has breathing room around it following the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a Z-pattern similar to the mockup: starting at the global logo, moving through the top navigation and search, then dropping to the left-aligned breadcrumb. From there, the eye is drawn diagonally down-right into the job hero: first to the large job title, then to the line(s) of metadata beneath it, and finally settling on the bold, red pill-shaped 'Apply with saved profile' button centered or slightly right-weighted under the text.
