Scrolling down, the flow becomes a straight vertical path: job description first (read the details), then the application form (take action), with any status or error banners interrupting above these sections so they are noticed immediately. The hierarchy ensures the user understands they are on a job detail, what the job is, and how to apply, in that order.

layout.overall_description: The page reads like a focused marketing detail view framed within the association’s global header and hero style. After the fixed header with logo, navigation, and search, the content area opens with a narrow breadcrumb row pinned to the left within a centered, 12-column container. Immediately below, a full-width hero band introduces the job: a clean background (either a softened climbing-related image or a solid brand color) spans edge to edge, while the job’s title, organization, and key metadata sit centered horizontally within the middle 50–60% of the page. The job title dominates in large, high-contrast type, with compact metadata just below it, and a strong pill-shaped red CTA button positioned directly under the text stack, visually anchored a bit below the center of the hero height. As the user scrolls, the hero gives way to a white background main content area. First comes the job description as a single, readable column aligned to the same center grid, followed by a card-like application section that visually pops from the background with subtle shadow or border and comfortable internal padding. Status and error banners slide into reserved horizontal stripes near the top of the content column, ensuring that any application feedback appears before or just above the job description and application form. The overall effect is a calm, professional page that mirrors the marketing hero but repurposes it to sell a single job opportunity and guide the user smoothly into the application flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-detail-data-loader is not visually rendered but conceptually occupies the entire main content region beneath the global header, spanning the same centered content container width as the job detail sections. While data is loading, it can trigger placeholder elements (such as a skeleton version of the job-detail-header and job-description-section) that appear in the hero and body positions they will eventually occupy. These placeholders maintain the same vertical stacking order: breadcrumb first, then hero header area, then body sections, preserving the page’s spatial hierarchy even in loading states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the content container; controls the presence of loading states for all job detail components without affecting their geometric layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-detail-header occupies a full-width hero band directly below the breadcrumb row, extending across the entire viewport but constrained to a centered content container for its text. Vertically, this hero takes roughly the upper half of the visible content area (about 45–55% of the viewport height on desktop), creating a prominent introduction to the job. Inside, the job title is centered horizontally within the central 6–8 columns of the 12-column grid, with large, bold type. It sits in the upper-middle of the hero, with generous vertical spacing above and below. Immediately below the title, the job-detail-metadata is aligned to the same center column, with slightly smaller type. The apply-with-saved-profile button is visually anchored below the metadata, centered or slightly right of center, leaving ample breathing room above and below so the hero feels open and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width hero section that scrolls with the page. On narrower screens, the hero collapses in height slightly but preserves the centered stacking of title, metadata, and CTA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-detail-metadata appears inside the hero band, directly beneath the job title, aligned to the same center column. It spans roughly 60–70% of the content container width, centered, with individual metadata items (e.g., location, employment type, salary range, posting date) arranged in one or two horizontal rows. Each item is separated by consistent gaps (16–24px) and may use pill-shaped or lightly framed labels that echo the pill CTA style while remaining subtler in color. There is clear vertical spacing of about one line-height above and below the metadata group so it sits comfortably between the job title above and the CTA button below. On mobile, the metadata wraps gracefully into multiple rows but stays centered below the title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the hero; adapts responsively by wrapping content but maintains its position between the title and the main CTA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-description-section is the first main body block beneath the hero (and any status banner), starting after a generous vertical gap of about 48px. It is contained within the same centered content container, spanning roughly 8–10 of the 12 columns to keep line length comfortable. The section begins with a clear section heading (e.g., 'Job Description') aligned left within the container, followed by well-spaced paragraphs and lists describing responsibilities, requirements, and benefits. Internally, subsections are separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing, ensuring a readable, low-density feel. This section occupies approximately the upper half of the post-hero body on initial scroll and may extend further down if the content is long, always maintaining consistent left and right margins aligned with the hero text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked block; expands vertically based on content while keeping a fixed maximum width in the center of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-navigation-breadcrumb sits immediately beneath the global header and above the job-detail-header hero, occupying a shallow horizontal strip. It is left-aligned within the centered content container, starting at the first column of the 12-column grid and spanning about 6–8 columns, leaving whitespace to the right. The breadcrumb text is small and understated, with 8–16px of vertical padding above and below, visually separated from the hero by about 16–24px of space. This placement ensures that as soon as the user looks below the site header, they see where they are in the site hierarchy before focusing on the hero’s job title and CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the top of the content. It scrolls away with the page and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The apply-with-saved-profile-button is the primary CTA inside the job-detail-header hero. It is a pill-shaped, bold red button positioned directly beneath the job-detail-metadata, centered horizontally within the hero’s content area. The button spans roughly 25–35% of the content container width on large screens, with generous horizontal padding (16–32px) and comfortable vertical padding (8–16px), making it visually substantial but not oversized. There is ample whitespace (24–32px) above and below the button, so it sits as a distinct focal point near the lower center of the hero band. On scroll, this CTA naturally leads into the application form below, with the interaction possibly scrolling the user down to the saved-profile-application-form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; maintains central alignment and pill shape responsively, shrinking in width on smaller screens but always occupying a prominent central position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-profiles-data-loader operates within the vertical space reserved for the application section, directly below the job-description-section. While profiles are loading, it can show inline skeleton elements or a loading indicator where the saved-profile-selection-panel will appear, centered within the application form card. The loader’s effective footprint matches the width of the application section (about 8–10 columns centered), ensuring that the perceived structure of the page remains consistent even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, confined to the application section’s width; swaps itself out for the selection panel once data is ready without changing the overall layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-profile-selection-panel appears at the top of the application area within the saved-profile-application-form container, beneath a section title such as 'Apply with a saved profile.' It spans the full internal width of the form card (about 8–10 columns in the centered grid), with 24–32px of padding around its contents. Profile options may be listed as stacked rows or pill-like cards, aligned left, with consistent vertical spacing (12–16px) between each option. The panel is visually grouped with a subtle border or background contrast to distinguish it from the rest of the form while still feeling part of the same card. It occupies roughly the upper third of the application section height, leaving room below for the message field and submission controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, nested within the application form card; expands vertically as the number of profiles grows, pushing the message field and submit area downward while preserving the card’s margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-message-field sits directly beneath the saved-profile-selection-panel within the application form card, separated by about 16–24px of vertical spacing. It spans the full internal width of the card, with a multi-line text area that visually occupies roughly 40–60% of the card’s height when first presented, giving users a clear impression that a thoughtful message is expected. The label and helper text (if present) are left-aligned and use the same grid alignment as other form elements, with generous spacing above and below. The field’s height is sufficient to show several lines of text without scrolling, maintaining the spacious, low-density feel consistent with the overall design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the application form; the text area can grow vertically as the user types, pushing subsequent form elements downward while maintaining container padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-profile-application-form is a distinct section directly below the job-description-section, separated by about 48px of vertical whitespace. It appears as a centered card-like container spanning roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns, with 24–32px internal padding and subtle shadow or border to differentiate it from the white page background. The form’s internal layout is vertically stacked: a section heading at the top, then the saved-profile-selection-panel, followed by the application-message-field, and finally the submit control row (which aligns left or center near the bottom). Each internal block is separated by consistent vertical spacing (16–24px), preserving the spacious marketing feel. The form as a whole fills the lower portion of the page after the description, creating a clearly defined action zone that feels as important as the hero CTA but more detailed and task-oriented.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked block; its card width stays fixed relative to the content container while its height grows with form content. On mobile, it stretches closer to full width but retains internal padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-status-banner is a horizontal notification band that appears just below the hero (job-detail-header) and above the job-description-section within the centered content container. It spans the same width as the main content (about 8–10 grid columns) and uses a high-contrast background color (e.g., green or neutral) with ample vertical padding (12–16px) to make it noticeable without overpowering the hero. The text is left-aligned, with optional status icon on the left, and there is 16–24px of whitespace above and below the banner, clearly separating it from surrounding sections. When present, it slightly compresses the whitespace between hero and body, but it does not overlap or obscure any other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline banner; appears and disappears in place, pushing adjacent content up or down but never overlaying or floating above other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-state-guard conceptually wraps the entire application area—from the hero CTA through to the saved-profile-application-form—controlling which application-related components are visible based on the user’s progress (e.g., not yet applied, pending, completed). It does not render a visible UI element itself but determines when to show or hide the apply-with-saved-profile-button, the application-status-banner, and the saved-profile-application-form. Spatially, it ensures that any substitutions (for example, replacing the form with a confirmation message) occur in the same reserved vertical spaces so the page’s structure remains familiar and stable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, logical wrapper; toggles visibility of application components while preserving their spatial footprints to avoid jarring layout shifts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job-detail-error-state appears when the job data cannot be loaded or the job is unavailable. In this state, it replaces the standard hero and body content within the main content area. The error message is centered both horizontally and vertically within the visible portion of the content container beneath the global header, occupying roughly the central 50% of the viewport height. The message block spans about 6–8 columns, with a clear headline, explanatory text, and any action buttons (e.g., 'Back to job listings') stacked with generous vertical spacing. Surrounding whitespace is substantial, mirroring the hero’s openness so the error feels intentional and calm rather than cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the content container when active; it pushes or replaces other job-detail components rather than overlaying them, ensuring a simple, focused error view.

name: Courses Listing
title: Courses
description: Interactive catalog of association courses where users can filter, sort, and browse courses, then open a specific course detail page for more information and enrollment.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for courses by calling getCourseFilterOptions and bind them to filter and sort controls.
Initialize filter and sort state from URL parameters (if present) and use that state to call searchCourses to retrieve the initial list of courses.
Allow users to filter courses by level, maximum duration, minimum rating, and category using options provided by getCourseFilterOptions, then refresh results via searchCourses.
Allow users to change the sort order using sort options from getCourseFilterOptions, updating the URL and reloading results via searchCourses.
Support paginated browsing of results using the limit and offset parameters of searchCourses, with the current page encoded in URL parameters.
Render course cards based on Course data returned from searchCourses and allow navigation to course_detail.html with the selected courseId when a card is clicked.
Handle empty-result scenarios from searchCourses by displaying an informative message and offering quick ways to reset or adjust filters.
user_workflows:
Browse all courses from navigation:
1. User navigates to courses.html without query parameters.
2. Page calls getCourseFilterOptions to obtain available levels, duration options, rating thresholds, categories, and sort options.
3. Page sets default filter state (no constraints) and default sort (taken from the sort options list) because there are no URL query values.
4. Page calls searchCourses with the default filters, sort, configured page size as limit, and offset 0.
5. The course list, summary, and pagination controls render based on the searchCourses response.
6. User scrolls the list, changes page if desired, or clicks a course to open its detail page.

Arrive with pre-applied filters from index.html:
1. From index.html, the user clicks a link to courses.html that includes one or more query parameters (such as level, max_duration_weeks, min_rating, category, or sort).
2. On load, the page reads these URL parameters and maps them to its internal filter and sort state.
3. Page calls getCourseFilterOptions and aligns the initial control selections with any matching URL values (ignoring any that do not match available options).
4. Page calls searchCourses using the derived filter and sort state.
5. The user sees courses already filtered and sorted according to the incoming URL values and can further refine filters or adjust sorting.
6. Any subsequent filter or sort changes update the URL and trigger new searchCourses calls.

Refine courses using filters:
1. User uses the filter controls (level, maximum duration, minimum rating, category) to change one or more values.
2. Filter component emits a filter-change event with the new filter state.
3. URL state manager updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new filter values (removing parameters when filters are cleared).
4. URL state manager resets pagination to the first page and calls searchCourses with the updated filters, current sort, configured limit, and offset 0.
5. Course list, summary, and pagination components update based on the new results.
6. User can repeat this process until an appropriate set of courses is displayed.

Change sorting of courses:
1. User selects a different sort option from the sort control.
2. Sort control emits a sort-change event with the selected sort value.
3. URL state manager updates the sort parameter in the URL and resets pagination to the first page.
4. URL state manager calls searchCourses with the same filters, new sort value, configured limit, and offset 0.
5. Course list, summary, and pagination components refresh to reflect the new ordering.

Navigate through pages of results:
1. User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page number).
2. Pagination component emits a page-change event with the new page index.
3. URL state manager calculates the corresponding offset from the page index and page size, then updates a pagination-related URL parameter (for example, page or offset).
4. URL state manager calls searchCourses with the current filters, current sort, configured limit, and the new offset.
5. Course list and summary update to show courses for the selected page.

Open a course detail page from a course card:
1. User clicks anywhere on a course card that is designated as the primary interaction area.
2. Course list component reads the Course.id associated with that card.
3. Component triggers navigation to course_detail.html with courseId in the query string.
4. Browser loads course_detail.html?courseId=<selectedCourse.id>, where the detail page can then load and display the specific course.
interactions: Initial load without URL parameters: on page load, the URL state manager reads the URL (finding no recognized query parameters), calls getCourseFilterOptions to populate filter and sort controls, computes default filter/sort state, and then calls searchCourses with default values.

Initial load with URL parameters: on page load, the URL state manager parses level, max_duration_weeks, min_rating, category, and sort from the URL, calls getCourseFilterOptions, aligns initial control selections to any matching options, then calls searchCourses with the mapped parameters.

Selecting a level filter: when the user chooses a level option, the filter component updates its internal state, emits an onLevelChange event with the new value, the URL state manager updates the level URL parameter (or removes it if cleared), resets pagination, and calls searchCourses with the updated level parameter.

Selecting a maximum duration filter: when the user chooses a duration option, the filter component emits an onDurationChange event, the URL state manager updates max_duration_weeks in the URL, resets pagination, and calls searchCourses with the new maxDurationWeeks value.

Selecting a minimum rating filter: when the user chooses a rating threshold, the filter component emits an onRatingChange event, the URL state manager updates min_rating in the URL, resets pagination, and calls searchCourses with the new minRating value.

Selecting a category filter: when the user chooses a category, the filter component emits an onCategoryChange event, the URL state manager updates category in the URL, resets pagination, and calls searchCourses with the new category value.

Resetting all filters: when the user triggers a reset action in the filter component, the component emits an onResetFilters event, the URL state manager clears all relevant filter-related URL parameters, resets pagination and sort to defaults, and calls searchCourses with no filter constraints.

Changing sort order: when the user selects a new sort option, the sort-control component emits an onSortChange event, the URL state manager updates the sort parameter in the URL, resets pagination, and calls searchCourses with the new sortBy value.

Paginating forward or backward: when the user selects next/previous, pagination component emits onNextPageClick or onPreviousPageClick with the new page index, the URL state manager updates a pagination parameter in the URL, calculates the new offset, and calls searchCourses with unchanged filters/sort and the new offset.

Jumping to a specific page: when the user clicks a specific page number, pagination component emits onPageNumberClick with that page index, the URL state manager updates the pagination parameter, computes offset, and calls searchCourses accordingly.

Handling empty results: when searchCourses returns totalCount equal to zero, the empty-state component becomes visible, offering an action (such as clearing filters) that emits onClearFiltersClick; the URL state manager responds by clearing filter parameters from the URL and calling searchCourses again with default filter state.

Clicking a course card: when the user clicks a course card, the course-list component emits an onCardClick event with the associated Course.id and triggers browser navigation to course_detail.html with a courseId query parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter, sort, and pagination state. On every load or relevant interaction, the URL state manager reads and writes these parameters and coordinates calls to getCourseFilterOptions and searchCourses.

Parameter mapping from URL to searchCourses parameters:
- level (URL) → level (searchCourses): If present in the URL and if it matches one of the level values obtained from getCourseFilterOptions, it becomes the selected level filter and is passed to searchCourses as the level argument. If absent or not matching available options, level is omitted or set to an unfiltered state for searchCourses.
- max_duration_weeks (URL) → maxDurationWeeks (searchCourses): If present and valid, it becomes the maximum duration constraint and is passed as maxDurationWeeks. If not present, the duration filter is not applied in searchCourses.
- min_rating (URL) → minRating (searchCourses): If present and valid, it becomes the minimum rating constraint and is passed as minRating. If not, this filter is not applied.
- category (URL) → category (searchCourses): If present and matching one of the category values from getCourseFilterOptions, it becomes the selected category filter and is passed as category. Otherwise, category is not constrained in searchCourses.
- sort (URL) → sortBy (searchCourses): If present and matching one of the sort option values from getCourseFilterOptions, it becomes the sortBy argument in searchCourses. If missing or invalid, the default sort is taken from the getCourseFilterOptions.sortOptions list and used for sortBy, and the URL may be updated to include it for consistency.

Pagination-related state:
- The page uses a configured page size (page size is treated as a configurable constant and not hardcoded here). This value is passed as limit to every searchCourses call.
- A pagination-related URL parameter (such as page index or offset) is used to represent the current position in the result set. From this parameter and the configured page size, the URL state manager computes the offset value passed to searchCourses. For example, if a page index is used, offset = (pageIndex - 1) * pageSize.
- When any filter or sort parameter changes, the pagination parameter is reset (e.g., page index set to the first page), and offset is recalculated accordingly.

URL updates and reactivity:
- Any user interaction that changes filter, sort, or pagination state triggers an update to the URL query string. Parameters for filters or sort that are cleared are removed from the URL to avoid stale state.
- After writing to the URL, the URL state manager calls searchCourses with parameters derived from the updated URL and current configuration (limit and offset). This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL will reproduce the same view.
- The URL state manager listens for browser navigation events (such as back/forward). When such an event occurs and the URL changes, it re-parses the query parameters and calls searchCourses again with the parameters represented in the URL, so the visible filters, sort control, pagination, and course list always reflect the URL.

Interaction with getCourseFilterOptions:
- getCourseFilterOptions is called on initial load (and can be reused as long as the page is active) to retrieve the list of possible values for levels, duration options, rating thresholds, categories, and sort options.
- The results of getCourseFilterOptions are used to validate incoming URL parameters. If a URL parameter does not correspond to a valid option, it is ignored in the initial filter state, and the invalid value may be removed from the URL when the first user-triggered state change occurs.
- The default selections for filters and sorting are determined by the options returned by getCourseFilterOptions and are applied only when no valid URL parameters are present for that control.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads and writes URL parameters, coordinates calls to getCourseFilterOptions and searchCourses, and distributes the resulting state to other components. On initial load, it parses query parameters, calls getCourseFilterOptions, determines initial filter and sort state, computes pagination state (limit and offset), and calls searchCourses. On subsequent interactions (filter changes, sort changes, pagination changes, or browser navigation), it updates the URL and reissues searchCourses with parameters derived from the URL.

Renders interactive controls for filtering courses by level, maximum duration, minimum rating, and category. It receives available options from getCourseFilterOptions via the controller and initial selected values based on URL parameters. Each change to a filter emits an event back to the controller, which updates URL parameters and triggers a new searchCourses call. It also provides a reset action to clear all active filters.

Displays the list of sort options obtained from getCourseFilterOptions and indicates the currently active sort order, which is derived from the sort URL parameter or defaulted by the controller. When the user selects a sort option, it emits an event that causes the controller to update the sort parameter in the URL, reset pagination, and call searchCourses with the new sortBy value.

Shows a concise summary of the current search state using data from searchCourses responses and the current pagination state, such as how many courses are being displayed out of the total and which page is in view. It updates whenever new results are loaded or pagination changes.

Displays the list of courses returned by searchCourses as a set of course cards. Each card binds directly to fields from the Course model (such as title, rating information, duration, formats, level, and category) as provided by searchCourses. The component updates whenever the controller provides new course data. Clicking on a card triggers navigation to course_detail.html with a courseId query parameter based on the Course.id.

Renders controls for navigating between result pages based on totalCount from searchCourses and the configured page size. It reflects the current page derived from URL parameters and emits events when the user requests next, previous, or a specific page. The controller then updates the URL and calls searchCourses with a recalculated offset.

Displays an informative message when searchCourses returns no courses (totalCount equals zero). It optionally provides an action to clear or relax filters. When this action is triggered, it signals the controller to reset filter-related URL parameters and re-run searchCourses without constraints, allowing the user to restart their search.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. Header content (logo, nav, search) is aligned along this grid with clear left, center, and right zones.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored on a centered content container that follows a 12-column grid. The global header and hero remain full-width at the top (matching the mockup). Immediately below the hero, the courses page introduces a horizontal control band that combines the results summary on the left and sort control on the right, echoing the header’s left/right balance. Beneath this band, the main content splits into a two-column layout: a vertical filter bar on the left and a course grid on the right. Pagination or a no-results state appears at the bottom of the grid, maintaining the vertical flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored on a centered content container that follows a 12-column grid. The global header and hero remain full-width at the top (matching the mockup). Immediately below the hero, the courses page introduces a horizontal control band that combines the results summary on the left and sort control on the right, echoing the header’s left/right balance. Beneath this band, the main content splits into a two-column layout: a vertical filter bar on the left and a course grid on the right. Pagination or a no-results state appears at the bottom of the grid, maintaining the vertical flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the hero, the control band (summary + sort) occupies the full width of the content container, but is relatively shallow (about 10–15% of the viewport height), serving as a clean transition into the catalog. The main content area under it uses roughly a 3/12–4/12 width (about 25–30%) for the left filter bar and 8/12–9/12 width (about 70–75%) for the course grid on the right. Vertically, the course grid and its controls occupy the majority of the scrollable space, with generous 48–64px vertical gaps separating key sections (hero → controls, controls → content, content → pagination).
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately spacious density similar to the mockup: controls and cards are not crowded, and there is clear separation between functional groups. The filter bar uses stacked sections with 24–32px spacing between groups. The control band has ample padding (around 24px top and bottom) and only a few concise pieces of information. The course cards are sized to show an image, title, key metadata, and primary CTA without feeling cramped, with consistent inner padding around 24px and 24–32px gaps between cards.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a familiar F/Z pattern: after the global header and hero, attention lands at the left side of the control band (results summary), then moves horizontally to the right (sort control). From there, the eye drops down along the left edge to discover the vertical filter bar, then shifts right into the more visually dominant course grid. Within each row, cards are scanned left-to-right, and at the bottom the eye is drawn to centered pagination controls. When there are no results, the visual flow is intercepted mid-column by a centered empty-state message, clearly indicating the lack of content and suggesting remedial actions.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width header and photographic hero, the courses listing page begins with a clean, full-width content band centered on the page. This band houses a concise results summary aligned to the left and a pill-shaped sort control aligned to the right, mirroring the balanced, horizontal structure of the main navigation above. A generous slice of whitespace separates this band from the main catalog area, which splits into a calm two-column layout: a taller, card-like filter bar running down the left third of the content, and a grid of course cards occupying the remaining two-thirds on the right. The filters are visually sturdy but not overwhelming, using solid color blocks and pill-shaped chips that echo the brand’s CTA styling. The course grid is the clear hero of this section, with evenly spaced cards in rows, each given room to breathe and framed by consistent padding. At the very bottom, centered pagination controls form a neat, pill-based cluster that caps the page and encourages further exploration. When the selected filters yield no matches, the right-column grid gracefully transforms into a spacious empty state, vertically centered within that column and framed by whitespace, ensuring the message is unmistakable yet still aligned with the overall clean, modern aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is a non-visual controller attached to the main courses page container. It occupies no visible space in the layout and does not affect the grid or spacing. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the page’s logic layer, listening to changes in filters, sorting, and pagination and synchronizing those states with the URL query parameters. It scrolls along as part of the document structure but has no width, height, or visual presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-interactive; logically fixed to the page-level context but with no rendered footprint or positional behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course filter bar appears below the summary/sort control band, occupying the left column of the main content area. Within the centered 12-column container, it spans approximately 3 of 12 columns (about 25% of the content width) and extends vertically down the page as a tall card-like panel. It is aligned flush with the left edge of the grid container, with a 48px vertical gap separating it from the controls band above. The filter bar has a solid, lightly contrasted background block with 24–32px internal padding on all sides and rounded corners to echo the pill-shaped design language. Filter groups are stacked vertically inside, each with its own heading at the top and 16–24px spacing between the group heading and its controls (checkboxes, pills, sliders, etc.). Individual filter controls use generous hit areas (around 8–12px vertical padding) and are separated by 8–16px gaps to preserve clarity. Horizontally, the filter bar’s right edge aligns with the column boundary, leaving a clear visual gutter of at least 24px before the start of the course grid on the right. On taller pages, the filter bar appears to anchor the left side of the layout, visually balanced by the grid to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content; on larger viewports it may behave as a sticky column, remaining pinned near the top of the viewport while the course list scrolls, as long as it fits within the viewport height. On smaller screens, it can collapse into a slide-down or overlay panel triggered from a pill-shaped 'Filters' control at the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course sort control sits within the horizontal control band immediately beneath the hero and page title area. This band spans the full width of the centered content container (roughly 80–90% of the page width), with 24px top and bottom padding and 16–24px left and right padding. The sort control is right-aligned within this band, occupying roughly 15–20% of the band’s width, and presented as a pill-shaped dropdown or segmented control. It aligns horizontally with the baseline of the results summary text on the left, producing a clean left-right tension reminiscent of the header’s logo and search alignment. There is ample whitespace around the sort control, with at least 16px gap from the container edge and 24px separation from any adjacent controls, emphasizing its importance without making it dominant. The pill is visually emphasized with a subtle border or filled background that uses brand colors, yet it remains lighter in weight than the primary CTAs elsewhere on the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element anchored to the top-right of the content control band; it scrolls away with the band as the user moves down the course list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course results summary is positioned at the top of the main content area, directly below the hero and page heading, forming the left portion of a full-width horizontal control band. This band is centered in the viewport and constrained to the main content width (aligned to the 12-column grid). The summary occupies roughly the left 60–70% of this band, starting from the left container padding and extending toward the center. It consists of a single line or two of concise text (e.g., '24 courses • Filtered by: Beginner, Weekends') set in medium-to-large type, with generous line height, reflecting the mockup’s preference for readable, minimally dense text. The summary text is aligned left, with about 24px padding above and below, and sits on a slightly tinted background that differentiates the band from the white page background. This band creates a visual bridge between the hero above and the filter/grid layout below, using whitespace (around 32–48px) below it before the two-column content begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band at the top of the main content section; it scrolls off the screen as the user moves down the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course list occupies the main right-hand content column beneath the control band, sitting to the right of the filter bar. Within the 12-column grid, it spans approximately 9 of 12 columns (about 70–75% of the content width). There is a consistent horizontal gutter of 24–32px separating its leftmost cards from the filter bar. Courses are arranged in a responsive grid of cards with 3 cards per row on large screens (each roughly 4 columns wide) and 2 per row on medium screens, maintaining the center-aligned content feel. Each card has a clean, modern look: an image block at the top, followed by the course title, a short description or key details, and a primary CTA (e.g., 'View Course' or 'Enroll'), all contained within a subtly elevated card with 24px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between text elements. Vertical spacing between rows of cards is around 32px to preserve the layout’s openness. The entire grid starts about 32–48px below the bottom edge of the control band, so the transition from controls to content feels comfortable and consistent with the mockup’s spacious rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content that expands vertically as more courses load or are paginated. Cards wrap to new rows based on available width while maintaining consistent gutters and alignment with the grid system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed directly below the course list, centered within the same right-hand content column that contains the grid. They are separated from the last row of course cards by a generous 32–48px vertical gap, creating a clear end to each page of results. The control group itself forms a compact horizontal strip of pill-shaped buttons (e.g., previous, page numbers, next) with equal spacing between each pill (about 8–16px). The entire pagination cluster occupies roughly the central 40–50% of the column’s width, leaving comfortable whitespace to the left and right so it doesn’t feel cramped or edge-to-edge. The primary active page and next/previous controls use the brand’s stronger color accent, while inactive page numbers are more subdued but still clearly interactive. Vertically, there is an additional 48–64px spacing below the pagination to signal the natural end of the page content before the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the bottom of the course list in the normal document flow; it scrolls off-screen along with the content as users move back up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results state appears in place of the course grid and pagination when the active filters produce no matching courses. It is positioned within the same right-hand content column that normally holds the course list, aligned with the top edge where the first row of cards would appear. Instead of multiple cards, a single, spacious empty-state panel is centered horizontally within this column and given a generous vertical footprint. This panel uses a light background block with 32px internal padding and rounded corners, echoing the card styling but scaled up. At the top of the panel, a simple, on-brand illustration or icon sits centered, followed by a clear, concise heading (e.g., 'No courses match these filters') and a short explanatory line. Below that, one or two pill-shaped actions (such as 'Clear filters' or 'Adjust filters') are centered and spaced with 16–24px gaps. Surrounding whitespace (at least 48px above and below) ensures the empty state reads as a distinct, intentional message rather than a glitch. The filter bar remains visible on the left, reinforcing that users can adjust their selections to restore results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered content that replaces the course grid and pagination within the same flow position. It scrolls normally with the page and remains aligned to the right-hand column layout.

name: Course Detail & Enrollment
title: Course Detail & Enrollment
description: Displays detailed information for a selected course, allows users to enroll in an available format, and lets users add the course to their personal learning plan.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific course identified by the courseId URL parameter using getCourseDetails.
Show course metadata such as level, duration, rating, available formats, and syllabus using the Course data model.
Allow the user to select an available course format (when multiple exist) based on formatOptions returned by getCourseDetails.
Provide an Enroll action that calls enrollInCourse with the chosen format, and show confirmation or error feedback based on the response.
Detect if the user is already enrolled in the course via getCourseDetails.isEnrolled and adapt the UI (e.g., disable enrollment, show enrollment status).
Display the user’s current learning plan information using getLearningPlan to determine whether the course is already in the plan.
Offer an Add to Learning Plan / In Learning Plan action that calls addCourseToLearningPlan and updates the learning plan state.
Provide navigation back to the course listing (courses.html) via a Back to Courses link and/or breadcrumbs.
user_workflows: View course details: 1) User navigates to course_detail.html with a courseId URL parameter. 2) On page load, the page reads courseId from the URL. 3) The page calls getCourseDetails(courseId). 4) In parallel, the page calls getLearningPlan(). 5) When getCourseDetails returns, the page displays the course title, description, level, duration, rating, available formats, and syllabus. 6) When getLearningPlan returns, the page checks whether the current courseId is in learningPlan.courseIds to update the learning plan action state.

Enroll in a course: 1) On the Course Detail & Enrollment page, the user reviews the course information. 2) If multiple formats are available in formatOptions, the user selects a preferred format through the format selector component; if only one format is available, it is preselected. 3) The user clicks the Enroll button. 4) The page validates that a chosenFormat is selected and that the course is active and not already enrolled. 5) The page calls enrollInCourse(courseId, chosenFormat). 6) While waiting for a response, the Enroll button shows a loading state and is disabled. 7) On success, the page reads courseEnrollment from the response, updates local enrollment state to reflect status=enrolled, may update the URL with an enrollmentStatus parameter (e.g., enrollmentStatus=success), and displays a confirmation message in the enrollment status component. 8) On failure, the page displays the message returned in the response and keeps the user on the same page.

Add course to learning plan: 1) On the Course Detail & Enrollment page, after details load, the page uses learningPlan.courseIds to determine whether the current course is already in the learning plan. 2) If not in the plan, the Add to Learning Plan button is enabled. 3) The user clicks Add to Learning Plan. 4) The page calls addCourseToLearningPlan(courseId). 5) While waiting for a response, the button shows a loading state and is disabled. 6) On success, the page reads learningPlan from the response and updates the local learning plan state, marking the course as included; the button label and state change accordingly and a confirmation message may appear. 7) If the course is already in the learning plan (detected either from the initial getLearningPlan response or from the updated learningPlan), the button is disabled or displays an in-plan state.

Return to course listing: 1) The user clicks the Back to Courses link or a breadcrumb that points to the courses.html page. 2) The page navigates to courses.html without additional processing on this page. 3) Optionally, if a source context is encoded in a URL parameter (e.g., from=index or from=courses) when arriving, that context can also be used in the breadcrumb to offer a link back to the originating page.

Revisit page after enrollment: 1) A user who previously enrolled returns to course_detail.html with the same courseId. 2) On load, getCourseDetails(courseId) returns isEnrolled=true. 3) The page shows the enrolled state (e.g., no active Enroll button, a message that the user is enrolled). 4) If the URL contains enrollmentStatus=success (for example, if set after a redirect or by updating the URL on success), the enrollment status component displays a one-time success message; if not, it simply shows a generic enrolled state.
interactions: On page load, read the courseId URL parameter and trigger data loading via getCourseDetails and getLearningPlan.
If courseId is missing or invalid, display an error state with a link back to courses.html.
Click Back to Courses or a breadcrumb link to navigate to courses.html.
Change the format selection using a radio button group or dropdown bound to formatOptions; updating the selected format in local state used by the enrollment form.
Click the Enroll button to submit the enrollment form, which triggers validation and then calls enrollInCourse with the selected format.

Handle successful enrollment by updating local enrollment state from the courseEnrollment response, optionally updating the URL (e.g., adding enrollmentStatus=success), and rendering a confirmation message.
Handle enrollment errors by displaying the message from the enrollInCourse response and allowing the user to retry.
Click the Add to Learning Plan button to call addCourseToLearningPlan, updating the learning plan state upon success and changing the button to an in-plan state.
Handle Add to Learning Plan errors by displaying the returned message and keeping the button enabled for retry.
Display loading indicators in place of the course details and learning plan actions while getCourseDetails and getLearningPlan calls are pending.
Display error messages if getCourseDetails or getLearningPlan fail, with an option to retry loading data (e.g., by re-triggering the interfaces).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary URL parameter is courseId. On initial load and any URL change, the page reads courseId. If courseId is absent or empty, the page shows a 'course not found' state and a Back to Courses navigation. If courseId is present, the page calls getCourseDetails(courseId) and getLearningPlan() in parallel. The result of getCourseDetails binds to the Course model and formatOptions and sets an isEnrolled flag. If getCourseDetails.isEnrolled is true, the enrollment panel renders in an 'already enrolled' state and disables the Enroll button; if false and Course.isActive is true, enrollment is allowed. The available formats are determined from the formatOptions array (and/or Course.formats); if the array length is 1, that format is preselected in local state; if multiple, the user must select one before enrolling. The LearningPlan model from getLearningPlan is used to compute a derived boolean such as isInLearningPlan by checking if the current courseId exists in learningPlan.courseIds; this boolean determines whether the Add to Learning Plan button is enabled. After a successful enrollInCourse call, the page updates local state to reflect that the user is now enrolled (isEnrolled=true) and stores the returned CourseEnrollment object to drive the enrollment status display; the page may add or update a URL parameter like enrollmentStatus=success or enrollmentStatus=error to persist feedback across reloads, but no state is stored in localStorage. After addCourseToLearningPlan returns successfully, the page replaces the current LearningPlan state with the returned learningPlan and recomputes isInLearningPlan, which will be true and will disable or change the Add to Learning Plan button. All transient UI state such as selected format, loading flags, and error messages is held in in-memory component state and is not persisted outside the page; only the courseId and any optional status parameters in the URL influence the persisted state behavior.

components.functionality: Controls initial page setup by reading the courseId from the URL, triggering getCourseDetails and getLearningPlan, managing loading and error states, and distributing loaded data (Course details, formatOptions, isEnrolled, LearningPlan) to child components.
Displays a simple breadcrumb trail including a link back to the course listing (courses.html) and the current course title, allowing the user to navigate out of the detail page.
Shows key course information such as title, short description, level, durationWeeks, rating, ratingCount, and category using values from the Course model.
Displays the course syllabus content from Course.syllabus, if available, allowing users to review the course outline before enrolling.

Renders available course formats (for example, from Course.formats and/or the formatOptions returned by getCourseDetails) and lets the user choose a single format to use as chosenFormat for enrollment.

Provides the Enroll button and handles enrollment logic, including validation that a format has been selected, checking isEnrolled and Course.isActive, calling enrollInCourse with courseId and chosenFormat, and handling loading, success, and error states.

Displays enrollment status messages based on current enrollment state (e.g., already enrolled, enrollment just succeeded, or enrollment error) using the CourseEnrollment status and any URL-based status flags.

Shows the Add to Learning Plan or In Learning Plan state for the current course. When clicked (and when the course is not already in the plan), it calls addCourseToLearningPlan with courseId, updates local learning plan data from the response, and shows feedback.
Displays messages related to learning plan operations, such as confirming that a course was added to the learning plan or showing an error returned by addCourseToLearningPlan.
Shows loading indicators for course details and learning plan information while getCourseDetails and getLearningPlan are in progress, and hides them once data is available or an error occurs.

Displays error messages when data loading or actions fail (for example, when getCourseDetails or getLearningPlan returns an error, or when enrollInCourse or addCourseToLearningPlan fail) and provides a retry control that re-triggers the appropriate interface via the page controller.
Provides a dedicated Back to Courses link that navigates the user to courses.html, used both in the normal page layout and in error or empty states.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. Header content (logo, nav, search) is aligned along this grid with clear left, center, and right zones.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: fixed full-width site header at the very top (logo, navigation, search) followed by a full-bleed course hero band that visually mirrors the marketing hero. Inside the hero, center a course summary block (course-summary-panel) that acts as the focal point. Directly beneath the hero, arrange the course body in a two-column grid: a wider left column for the syllabus and course details, and a narrower right column for enrollment controls and learning-plan actions. Breadcrumbs and back navigation sit at the top of the main content area, aligned to the left, just below the header and above the hero content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: fixed full-width site header at the very top (logo, navigation, search) followed by a full-bleed course hero band that visually mirrors the marketing hero. Inside the hero, center a course summary block (course-summary-panel) that acts as the focal point. Directly beneath the hero, arrange the course body in a two-column grid: a wider left column for the syllabus and course details, and a narrower right column for enrollment controls and learning-plan actions. Breadcrumbs and back navigation sit at the top of the main content area, aligned to the left, just below the header and above the hero content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height for the fixed header (existing site header) plus breadcrumbs/back link, and the remaining 80–85% for a visually dominant hero that introduces the course. Within the hero, dedicate approximately the central 50–60% of the content width to the course-summary-panel, leaving softer image space on either side. Below the hero, the main content uses a 12-column grid: the course-syllabus-section spans about 7–8 columns on the left, while the enrollment-panel (with format-selector, enrollment-status-banner, learning-plan-action, and learning-plan-status-message) occupies about 4–5 columns on the right. Vertical separation between major sections (breadcrumbs, hero, body content blocks) follows the 48–64px spacing rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the layout moderately spacious: generous margins around the hero and between sections, large type for headings, and concise text blocks. Panels like the course-summary-panel, enrollment-panel, and syllabus sections use 24–32px internal padding. Interactive elements (buttons, pills, inputs) follow the 8–16px vertical and 16–32px horizontal padding pattern, with pill-shaped buttons echoing the mockup. Avoid overly dense text; prioritize scannable headings and short paragraphs, especially around enrollment-related controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Maintain an F/Z-pattern: from the top-left logo across the header navigation to the top-right search, then down and back toward the left where the back-to-courses-link and breadcrumb-navigation begin the course context. From there, the eye drops into the center of the hero where the course-summary-panel headline and key information sit, then slightly right toward the enrollment-panel which carries the primary enrollment CTA. As users scroll, attention flows down through the left-column syllabus content while the sticky right-column enrollment panel keeps its CTA at peripheral but constant visibility. Status banners and messages use brand color accents to briefly interrupt and guide attention without overpowering the main CTAs.

layout.overall_description: The page sits beneath the site’s fixed, full-width header, which spans the entire top in a shallow horizontal band. Immediately below this header, the main content area begins with a narrow horizontal strip: in the left half, a subtle back-to-courses-link appears above a light, inline breadcrumb-navigation, both aligned to the same left margin of the centered content container. Below this, a full-bleed hero section fills most of the remaining initial viewport height. A large climbing-related photograph forms the background, softened toward the center to keep text legible. Centered within this hero, the course-summary-panel sits in the middle 50–60% of the width, vertically weighted toward the center of the hero band. It features a large course title, a brief description, and any key metadata, with generous spacing and a strong primary accent color echoing the brand’s bold marketing style. As the user scrolls below the hero, the layout transitions into a 12-column grid-based content band. On the left, the course-syllabus-section stretches across about two-thirds of the width as a stack of padded detail blocks, each separated by ample white space. On the right, beginning slightly higher than the first syllabus heading, a card-like enrollment-panel occupies the remaining one-third of the width. This panel has a clean, softly rounded rectangle shape with 24–32px padding and contains the enrollment-status-banner at the top, followed by a format-selector, the primary enrollment controls, the learning-plan-action, and the learning-plan-status-message. The enrollment panel behaves as a sticky card on taller viewports, staying visible as the syllabus scrolls underneath on the left. Loading and error states replace or overlay the main content area with centered, well-spaced indicators, preserving the tidy, modern marketing aesthetic even in edge cases.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual controller component governs the visibility of the course content area that begins just below the site header. When the course data is loading, it hides the hero, syllabus, and enrollment sections and instead shows the course-loading-state centered in the available viewport below the header. If an error occurs, it similarly hides the main course content and displays the course-error-state in its place. In the normal, loaded state, it reveals the full layout: breadcrumb-navigation and back-to-courses-link at the top, followed by the hero-like course-summary-panel and the two-column syllabus/enrollment region beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; controls conditional rendering of other components. Determines whether a centered loading or error overlay replaces the main content region beneath the fixed header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-navigation is positioned near the top of the main content area, directly beneath the site header and aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. It appears as a single horizontal line of breadcrumb links spanning roughly 50–60% of the page width, with subtle styling and light typography so it does not compete with the hero. It sits about 8–16px below the back-to-courses-link, with 16–24px of vertical space below it before the start of the hero (course-summary-panel background). On narrow screens, it wraps gracefully to a second line if needed, maintaining a left alignment and staying compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; scrolls away with the page content. It remains anchored to the top-left of the main content container and does not overlay the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course-summary-panel sits within a full-bleed hero band that stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport beneath the breadcrumb area. The hero’s height roughly fills the remaining initial viewport so that the course summary dominates the first screen. The background is a large, soft-focused climbing image; over this, the summary panel is centered horizontally in the middle 50–60% of the page width, with equal image breathing room on the left and right. Vertically, the panel is centered within the hero band, leaving comfortable top and bottom margins of roughly 15–20% of the hero height. Inside the panel, the course title is large and bold, spanning 2–3 lines with generous line spacing, followed by a short subtitle or description in smaller text, and key metadata (e.g., level, duration) arranged on one or two lines. These elements are stacked with 8–24px gaps between them. The text is either center-aligned or slightly left-aligned within the panel, but always visually grouped. The overall effect mimics the marketing hero: impactful, minimal, and focused on quickly communicating the value of the course.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero. It scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page, with no sticky behavior, and scales responsively to remain within the central portion of the viewport on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Below the hero, the course-syllabus-section starts roughly 48–64px beneath the bottom of the hero image, aligned to the left margin of the content container. It occupies about 7–8 columns of a 12-column grid on desktop, roughly 65–70% of the available content width, leaving space on the right for the enrollment-panel. The syllabus is presented as a vertically stacked series of sub-sections or modules, each within its own subtle block or separated by 24–32px of white space. Each syllabus block has a section title at the top, followed by brief descriptive text or bullet points, using a comfortable line height and 16–24px spacing between text groups to avoid clutter. On tablet and mobile viewports, the syllabus section shifts to full width and stacks above the enrollment panel, maintaining at least 32–48px of vertical separation between major sub-sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column. On larger screens it forms the left column; on smaller screens it becomes a full-width block that appears above the enrollment panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The format-selector lives inside the enrollment-panel, near its top, directly below the enrollment-status-banner. It spans nearly the full internal width of the enrollment card, leaving 16–24px of padding to the left and right edges. Visually, it is presented as either a pill-shaped dropdown or a horizontal group of pill buttons representing different course formats (e.g., in-person, online). The control is approximately one line tall but with generous vertical padding (8–12px) to match the pill aesthetic from the mockup. It has clear label text above or to the left with 8–12px spacing between label and control, ensuring good readability. On smaller screens when the enrollment-panel becomes full width, the format-selector stretches to 100% of the card’s content width, remaining one of the first focal elements within the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the enrollment-panel; its position is fixed relative to other elements in that card and it responds fluidly to card width changes across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The enrollment-panel is a prominent card situated to the right of the course-syllabus-section within the main content band below the hero. On desktop, it aligns with the top of the first visible syllabus heading (or slightly above by 16–24px), occupying about 4–5 columns of the 12-column grid, or roughly 30–35% of the content width. The panel has a white or very light background, a soft shadow or subtle border, and slightly rounded corners, creating a clear, elevated container. Internally, it uses 24–32px padding on all sides. Inside the card, the layout stacks vertically: the enrollment-status-banner at the very top, then a small gap (8–16px), then the format-selector, followed by the primary enrollment fields and the main enroll button (a bold, pill-shaped button that spans the full internal width). Below that, with 16–24px of separation, sits the learning-plan-action and then the learning-plan-status-message. Each element is spaced to avoid crowding, keeping the card clear and scannable. On tablet and mobile, this panel drops below the syllabus and expands to nearly full width with consistent padding and vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the panel behaves as a sticky right column: once the user scrolls past a threshold (e.g., 64px below the header), the card remains fixed within the viewport’s right side until the bottom of the main content is reached, ensuring the enrollment CTA is always visible as the syllabus scrolls. On smaller screens, the sticky behavior may be disabled, and the panel scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The enrollment-status-banner appears at the very top of the enrollment-panel, spanning its full internal width. It is a shallow horizontal strip (approximately 40–56px tall) with a background color that reflects the status (e.g., brand red for urgent/limited, green for open, gray for closed), using white or high-contrast text. The banner has rounded corners that match or slightly inset from the card’s rounding and is separated from the card’s edges by the panel’s padding (e.g., 24px left/right, 16–24px top). Text is left-aligned within the banner, with a compact line height but sufficient vertical padding (8–12px) to keep it comfortable. This banner is visually stronger than regular text but subordinate to the main enroll button, acting as a quick status cue at the top of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the top of the enrollment-panel; it scrolls with the card and remains visible when the card is sticky. It does not overlap other content and collapses vertically (or disappears) when not needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The learning-plan-action is positioned near the bottom of the enrollment-panel, below the primary enrollment controls and the main enroll button. It is left-aligned within the card and typically takes the form of a secondary pill-shaped button or link-styled button with an outline or lower-contrast fill, using the brand’s accent color but at a lighter intensity than the primary enroll CTA. It spans about 60–100% of the card’s content width depending on design choice, with horizontal padding of 16–24px and vertical padding of 8–12px. There is a clear vertical gap of 16–24px above it (from the primary CTA) and 8–16px below it (leading into the learning-plan-status-message), preserving hierarchy and readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the enrollment-panel; it moves with the panel as it becomes sticky on larger screens, staying visually tied to the enrollment area rather than floating independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The learning-plan-status-message appears directly beneath the learning-plan-action inside the enrollment-panel, left-aligned with the card’s content. It is a short line or two of smaller, subdued text that communicates the current relationship of the course to the user’s learning plan (e.g., already added, in progress). It uses a more muted color (e.g., mid-gray or a softer version of the accent color) to avoid competing with action items. There is a modest 8–16px vertical gap below the learning-plan-action and at least 16px of padding between the status message and the bottom edge of the card, so it feels anchored but not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the enrollment-panel; it may expand vertically for multi-line messages, pushing the bottom of the card downward, but it does not overlap other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the course-loading-state occupies the entire main content area beneath the site header, temporarily replacing the breadcrumb-navigation, hero, syllabus, and enrollment panel. It is centered both horizontally and vertically within this area, often presenting a spinner or progress indicator with a short status label below. The loading indicator’s width is small (no more than 15–20% of the viewport width), surrounded by generous white space on all sides (at least 64px to the nearest content edges), echoing the minimal, spacious feel of the mockup’s hero. The background remains the standard page background, keeping the experience calm and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an overlay or replacement for the main content region controlled by the course-detail-page-controller. It scrolls with the page but normally fits entirely within the initial viewport, minimizing the need to scroll while waiting.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When shown, the course-error-state replaces the main course content area beneath the header in the same space normally occupied by the hero and body. It appears as a centered card or message block approximately 40–60% of the viewport width, with 24–32px of internal padding and rounded corners, visually similar to other panels but with a stronger border or background accent indicating an error (e.g., light red). Inside, a clear error headline sits at the top, followed by a brief explanation and a suggested action (e.g., a retry button or a link back to the courses list). There is generous vertical whitespace surrounding the card (at least 48–64px from the top of the content area and between the card and the bottom of the viewport), ensuring the error state feels deliberate and not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the hero and body panels under the control of course-detail-page-controller. It scrolls as a normal content block but is sized to typically fit within the initial viewport height to keep the message immediately visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-courses-link is positioned at the very top of the main content area, directly below the site header and slightly above the breadcrumb-navigation. It is left-aligned with the primary content column and spans only as much width as needed for its text and icon (often a left chevron), leaving ample empty space to its right. The link uses a smaller or medium font size and a subdued but noticeable brand color, making it easy to spot without competing visually with the hero. Vertical spacing of about 8–12px separates it from the breadcrumbs below, and about 24–32px separates the breadcrumb line from the start of the hero section. On mobile, it remains at the top of the content stack, still left-aligned, providing a quick way to navigate back before any scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; scrolls away with the page content. It remains anchored to the top-left of the content container and does not become sticky.

name: Committees & Volunteering
title: Committees & Volunteering
description: Browse and filter association committees by category, meeting format, meeting time, and whether they are accepting volunteers. View committee summaries and navigate to detailed pages to learn more and get involved.

core_features: Load available filter options for committee category, meeting format, and meeting time using getCommitteeFilterOptions.
Initialize filter state from incoming URL parameters (category, meeting_format, meeting_time, only_accepting) and keep it in sync with the URL.
Search and filter committees based on current filter state using searchCommittees.
Display a dynamic list of committee summaries, including purpose, meeting cadence, basic requirements, meeting format/time, and whether they are accepting volunteers.
Provide clear calls to action on each committee result to navigate to the committee_detail.html page for more information and to volunteer.
Handle loading, empty results, and error states for both filter options and committee search.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open page without prefilters
1) User clicks 'Committees & Volunteering' in site navigation (no URL query parameters).
2) url-state-manager reads URL and initializes filter state with no selections and only_accepting unset.
3) filter-options-loader calls getCommitteeFilterOptions and passes the results to committee-filters-bar.
4) committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees with null/undefined filter values and onlyAcceptingVolunteers set according to default rules.
5) committee-list displays the returned committees; loading-error-indicator shows loading state until data is received.
6) User scans committee cards and clicks a card or its primary action button to navigate to committee_detail.html with committeeId in the query string.

Workflow 2: Open page with preselected filters from another page
1) User clicks a link on index.html or about.html pointing to committees.html with URL parameters (e.g., category, meeting_format, meeting_time, only_accepting).
2) url-state-manager parses the query parameters and initializes the filter state.
3) filter-options-loader calls getCommitteeFilterOptions and passes options plus the preselected filter values into committee-filters-bar.
4) committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees using the mapped filter values (category, meetingFormat, meetingTime, onlyAcceptingVolunteers) derived from URL state.
5) committee-list displays only committees matching the preselected filters.
6) User may refine filters; each change updates the URL parameters and triggers a new searchCommittees call.

Workflow 3: Refine filters to find a suitable committee
1) User adjusts the category, meeting format, meeting time, or only-accepting filter in committee-filters-bar.
2) committee-filters-bar emits a filter-change event to url-state-manager and committee-search-controller with the updated filter state.
3) url-state-manager updates the browser URL query parameters (category, meeting_format, meeting_time, only_accepting) to reflect the new state.
4) committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees with the updated filter values.
5) committee-list updates to show the new set of committees; if no results are returned, empty-state-message is shown with an option to reset or broaden filters.

Workflow 4: Clear filters and see all committees
1) User clicks a 'Reset filters' or equivalent action in committee-filters-bar or empty-state-message.
2) url-state-manager clears relevant query parameters from the URL.
3) committee-filters-bar resets its UI controls to their default (unselected) state.
4) committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees with no filter values (and default behavior for onlyAcceptingVolunteers).
5) committee-list displays the full set of committees.

Workflow 5: Proceed to committee detail to volunteer
1) From committee-list, user clicks on a committee card or a 'View details / Volunteer' action.
2) committee-navigation-handler constructs a URL to committee_detail.html with the selected committee's id as the committeeId parameter.
3) Browser navigates to committee_detail.html?committeeId=... where volunteering-specific interactions and submissions occur.
interactions: On page load, filter-options-loader automatically calls getCommitteeFilterOptions; loading-error-indicator shows a loading state until options are returned.
On page load or whenever URL changes programmatically, url-state-manager parses category, meeting_format, meeting_time, and only_accepting parameters and updates internal filter state.
When filter options are successfully loaded, committee-filters-bar renders selection controls populated from getCommitteeFilterOptions and pre-selects values based on current filter state.

When the user changes the category selection in committee-filters-bar, an onCategoryChange handler updates filter state, url-state-manager updates the category URL parameter, and committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees with the new category.

When the user changes the meeting format selection, an onMeetingFormatChange handler updates filter state, url-state-manager updates the meeting_format URL parameter, and committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees with the new meetingFormat value.

When the user changes the meeting time selection, an onMeetingTimeChange handler updates filter state, url-state-manager updates the meeting_time URL parameter, and committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees with the new meetingTime value.

When the user toggles the 'Only committees currently accepting volunteers' control, an onOnlyAcceptingToggle handler updates filter state, url-state-manager updates the only_accepting URL parameter (as 'true' or 'false'), and committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees with onlyAcceptingVolunteers set accordingly.

When the user clicks a 'Reset filters' control, an onResetFilters handler clears all filter state fields, url-state-manager removes related URL parameters, and committee-search-controller calls searchCommittees with default (unfiltered) values.

When searchCommittees is called, loading-error-indicator shows loading state for the results region; when the call succeeds, committee-list receives the committees array and renders cards; when it fails, an error message is displayed with an option to retry.
When the user clicks on a committee card or its primary CTA in committee-list, an onCommitteeCardClick handler navigates to committee_detail.html with the corresponding committeeId URL parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter state that must be shareable and restorable. On every initial load, url-state-manager reads category, meeting_format, meeting_time, and only_accepting from the URL, normalizes them, and initializes the filter state object. This filter state is passed to committee-filters-bar to set initial control values and to committee-search-controller to call searchCommittees. The mapping between URL parameters and interface parameters is: category (URL) -> category (searchCommittees parameter), meeting_format (URL) -> meetingFormat (searchCommittees parameter), meeting_time (URL) -> meetingTime (searchCommittees parameter), only_accepting (URL with 'true'/'false' string) -> onlyAcceptingVolunteers (searchCommittees boolean/flag parameter). When filters change via user interaction, url-state-manager updates the query string using pushState/replaceState so the current filter state is encoded in the URL at all times. Any navigation away and back, or sharing/bookmarking the URL, will rehydrate the same filters by re-parsing these parameters. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used; all persistence is through the URL and fresh calls to getCommitteeFilterOptions and searchCommittees.
